title: #eng-releases: 1771227606
preview:
Connor O'Brien: Pulling this thread together for internal release notes + follow-ups after the canary-caught regression yesterday. Goal: write-up for #eng + what we’re doing before we reopen the merge window.

Connor O'Brien: Source incident thread: https://slack.redwood.example/archives/incidents/p1771143201-real-regression-caught-pre-rollout

Incident Bot: (context) Incident 2026-02-15: perf-canary detected sustained p95 regression on top model during limited rollout. Auto-gate triggered. Rollout halted. Reverted runtime build.

Vanessa Ortiz: For release notes: this correlated w/ a fleet change in us-east-a (driver rollout) *and* runtime change. Canary showed both p95 up + tokens/sec down.

Noah Patel: Canary signal details (for notes):
- model: `redwood/llama-3-70b-instruct` (prod alias)
- region: us-east
- hw: H100-80GB
- observed: p95 e2e +18-22% (runtime-only +15%), tokens/sec -9-11%
- queueing time flat -> likely execution path
- kernel_tag changed due to auto-selection tweak in runtime build `rt-2026.02.15.3`

Connor O'Brien: Great. We should keep it high level for release notes but include links to dashboard/graphs.

Aisha Bello: Dashboard link for the write-up: https://grafana.redwood.example/d/perf-canary-model?var-model=llama-3-70b&var-region=us-east
(Panel to call out: p95 runtime-only + kernel tag overlay)

Rafael Mendes: From on-call side: paging did not fire (this was within phase-1 gates, not paging-enabled). Gate pause did exactly what we want. Mention that explicitly.

Allison Grant: +1 and please state “no customer impact” (limited rollout, gate blocked). Also note we’re keeping warning rules on but still tuning paging thresholds.

DeployBot: Rollback executed by @Connor O'Brien
- Service: redwood-runtime
- Region: us-east
- From: rt-2026.02.15.3
- To: rt-2026.02.12.1
- Status: SUCCESS

Mateo Alvarez: For the internal note: highlight that the canary prevented broad impact and that attribution was fast because of kernel tagging + queueing split. This is the story we want.

Hiro Tanaka: Root cause (tentative): kernel auto-selection picked a slower fused attention variant for the long-prefill bucket. Triggered by a heuristic change in 02.15.3. Fix is to pin previous kernel for that bucket on H100 until we re-benchmark.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduler wasn’t the culprit (batch size dist unchanged, queue time stable). Worth calling out so people don’t chase the wrong thing next time.

Logan Wright: Telemetry note: we saw ingestion lag ~3-4 min in the middle of the event. Didn’t affect gate decision but made graphs confusing. I’ll add an action item to alert on pipeline lag for canary metrics.

Connor O'Brien: Draft release note bullets (please nitpick):
1) 02/15 limited runtime rollout paused by perf-canary gate due to sustained perf regression on a top model (p95 latency up, tokens/sec down).
2) No customer impact; rollout halted before expansion.
3) Rapid attribution via runtime-only latency + kernel_tag; scheduler/queueing signals were stable.
4) Mitigation: rollback to previous runtime build; kernel heuristic change reverted/pinned pending benchmark validation.
5) Follow-ups: tighten gate criteria + add additional prefill/decode breakdown panels; track telemetry pipeline lag.

Marissa Cole: Add one line on rollout workflow: “Gates set to auto-pause expansion; manual approval required to resume.” Product wants that documented as expected behavior.

Paula Mendes: Also, can we add “what changed” in the release itself? i.e., “kernel auto-selection heuristic update” (without too much detail). Useful for audits.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE: +1 on explicitly noting this did *not* page. We should avoid setting the expectation that every gate event == on-call incident.

Quentin Harris: Model registry piece: was model alias stable? For notes, mention we were pinned to version `llama3-70b-instruct@2026-02-10` so it wasn’t a model rollout.

Noah Patel: Confirmed. Model version didn’t move. Canary baseline was keyed on model+version+hw+rt_build.

Connor O'Brien: Good catch, adding: “model version unchanged; regression isolated to runtime build change.”

Allison Grant: Action items should be concrete with owners. Suggest:
- (Hiro) revert/pin kernel heuristic
- (Noah) add gate rule to require N samples per bucket before triggering
- (Logan) telemetry lag alert
- (Connor) update release checklist to include “canary green for 60 min” before region expansion

Vanessa Ortiz: Also add one for fleet signals: we should surface “driver version” tag on the canary dashboard. Even if not RCA here, it’s a common confounder.

Logan Wright: I can help but that’s probably Fleet Telemetry. Vanessa can you take an owner for that and I’ll pair?

Vanessa Ortiz: Yep. I’ll open a ticket for driver tag -> runtime telemetry (or at least the canary label set) and coordinate w/ Omar.

Rafael Mendes: For completeness: runbook link we used during triage: https://confluence.redwood.example/display/SRE/Runbook+-+Perf+Regression+Canary+Alert
This should go in the notes as “triage path”.

Connor O'Brien: Ok I’ll post a short internal note to #eng + attach to the next release change log. Also updating the eng-releases checklist doc.

Mateo Alvarez: Please include a brief “what worked / what was confusing” section. Confusing bits: telemetry lag, and initial question “fleet vs runtime”. What worked: canary gate + kernel tags.

Chloe Martin: If you want this visible in Console release history, send me the final bullet list and I’ll add it to the internal-only “Ops Notes” section.

Connor O'Brien: Will do. Targeting EOD.

Paula Mendes: One more request: explicitly state criteria for resuming rollout (e.g., fix merged + staged perf-canary validation + 24h warning-only). Helps align stakeholders.

Connor O'Brien: Added as final bullet: “Resume requires (a) kernel pin fix shipped, (b) staging validation plan signed off, (c) perf-canary green 60 min in us-east phase-1, (d) SRE + runtime approval to expand.”

Aisha Bello: Works for me. :thumbsup:

Hiro Tanaka: :thumbsup: I’ll post an update once the pin is in and benchmarks rerun.

Connor O'Brien: Thanks all. I’ll circulate draft in this thread before posting to #eng.

Connor O'Brien: Will do. Targeting EOD.

Paula Mendes: One more request: explicitly state criteria for resuming rollout (e.g., fix merged + staged perf-canary validation + 24h warning-only). Helps align stakeholders.

Connor O'Brien: Added as final bullet: “Resume requires (a) kernel pin fix shipped, (b) staging validation plan signed off, (c) perf-canary green 60 min in us-east phase-1, (d) SRE + runtime approval to expand.”

Aisha Bello: Works for me. :thumbsup:

Hiro Tanaka: :thumbsup: I’ll post an update once the pin is in and benchmarks rerun.

Connor O'Brien: Thanks all. I’ll circulate draft in this thread before posting to #eng.

title: #architecture: 1770219804
preview:
Noah Patel: Dropping ADR here for review: short/long prompt-aware scheduling policy (two-queue + aging) for mixed workloads. Main goal: cut p95 tail without tanking throughput. Please focus on fairness + multi-tenant implications + gating requirements. Link: confluence://architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-short-long-prompt-scheduling-policy

Lena Fischer: tl;dr: classify reqs as short/long based on prompt tokens + predicted prefill cost, keep two queues, dispatch biased toward short w/ token budget + aging so long makes progress. Feature-flagged, defaults off.

Architecture Bot: ADR review opened by Noah Patel. Requested reviewers: @architecture-core @eng-sre

Sofia Ivanova: For context: this is prototype phase only. Not trying to productize tenant QoS yet, but we need to not accidentally create one (or create unfairness that trips up Dedicated customers).

Sean Gallagher: SRE hat: I’m mostly worried about “silent policy change” in multi-tenant hosted pools. If we bias shorts, long-context tenants could see degraded p95/p99 and we won’t notice unless we split metrics by class + tenant. Needs strong guardrails.

Noah Patel: +1. ADR currently says “fairness guardrails” but I want it explicitly defined. What’s the fairness objective? Token fairness? Request fairness? Time-based? Right now it reads like: “try to keep shorts fast, ensure longs don’t starve” which is… not a policy, it’s a hope :slightly_smiling_face:

Lena Fischer: Fair call. For prototype we’re optimizing user-visible latency (TTFT/p95) for “short” while guaranteeing bounded waiting for long via aging. We’re not guaranteeing strict per-tenant fairness. We can add explicit invariants:
- starvation bound: max time-in-queue for long < X (config)
- dispatch share floor for long when long queue depth > 0
- cap on consecutive short dispatch iterations

Hiro Tanaka: Kernel side question: does class selection affect kernel auto-selection? We’ve had issues where mixing seq lengths causes kernel thrash. If policy oscillates between short/long every iteration, we could make that worse.

Lena Fischer: We’re planning token budgeting per “scheduler iteration” so we can batch multiple shorts before switching. Also config “min_run_length” for a class to avoid oscillation. But yes, kernel selection stability is a risk.

Noah Patel: Multi-tenant: even if not “QoS product”, if hosted pool has two classes, we’re implicitly prioritizing one traffic type over another. If a single tenant floods shorts, they can starve others’ longs unless aging is global not per-tenant. How are we defining aging scope?

Sofia Ivanova: Current implementation in PR draft is global per-pool queues. Aging is per-request based on enqueue time, not per-tenant. That helps starvation but still means a tenant with lots of shorts increases contention overall.

Paula Mendes: Product/rollout perspective: we *need* this gated per pool + per model, and ideally per customer tier in Dedicated. We also need a comms plan if Dedicated customers see long-context slowdowns.

Sean Gallagher: Gating requirements (IMO non-negotiable):
1) feature flag default OFF
2) allowlist pools (canary only)
3) runtime config bounds (no “set bias=1000 and melt GPU”)
4) metrics split by class + overall (p50/p95/p99, queue time, TTFT)
5) rollback is “flip flag” + revert params, no deploy required

Allison Grant: On metrics: we should align with existing naming conventions. Please don’t ship ad-hoc labels that explode cardinality. If you want tenant visibility, we need to do it via sampled traces or aggregation by “pool/model/class”, not “customer_id”.

Noah Patel: +1 on cardinality. ADR should explicitly say “no per-tenant labels”. But we still need to reason about tenant fairness without measuring it directly. Maybe define success criteria at pool level and watch for regressions in long-class p95.

Lena Fischer: Agree. Proposed instrumentation:
- scheduler_queue_depth{class=short|long}
- scheduler_time_in_queue_ms_bucket{class=...}
- scheduler_dispatch_share{class=...}
- scheduler_starvation_events_total{class=long}
- ttft_ms{class=...} (already exists, we add class label w/ bounded cardinality)

Sean Gallagher: Make sure “class” label is strictly {short,long} (and maybe “unknown”) and not a freeform tier.

Hiro Tanaka: Also please gate “prefill/decode separation hooks” behind the same flag. We don’t want the refactor to change behavior when policy is off.

Sofia Ivanova: Yes. Plan is: with flag off, code path should behave exactly like today’s queue selection. We’ll add regression tests around batch composition and ordering.

Noah Patel: Another concern: this looks like SRPT-lite. In multi-tenant systems SRPT can be unfair under certain distributions. ADR should include explicit statement: “We’re not implementing SRPT; we’re implementing two-class scheduling with bounded starvation.” And list failure modes.

Lena Fischer: Will update ADR with a section:
- Not SRPT (no continuous reordering by remaining tokens)
- Risks: long degradation, kernel thrash, cache eviction, oscillation
- Mitigations: token budgets, min-run, aging, caps

Connor O'Brien: Release perspective: if behavior changes even with flag off, we can’t land it right before runtime cut. Please keep “no-op when disabled” super tight.

Allison Grant: Re dashboards: we should require a pre-defined dashboard before canary. Otherwise SRE is blind. Can you add “dashboard exists” as a rollout prerequisite in ADR?

Sean Gallagher: Yes please. Also a rollback trigger: e.g. if overall p95 or long-class p95 increases > N% for > M minutes, auto page + flip flag.

Mateo Alvarez: Exec sponsor view: I’m okay taking some risk in canary as long as the blast radius is controlled. But I want the ADR to be crisp about what’s gated and what the “kill switch” is. And we should call out that “multi-tenant QoS is out of scope” explicitly so we don’t accidentally promise it.

Noah Patel: Summarizing required ADR edits from this thread:
- Define fairness objective + explicit invariants (starvation bound, dispatch floor/caps)
- Clarify aging scope (per-request, global queue)
- Explicit gating: flag default OFF, allowlist pools, per-model/pool params, bounded config
- “No-op when disabled” requirement (prefill/decode refactor included)
- Observability requirements: per-class metrics with bounded cardinality + dashboard prerequisite
- Rollback triggers + kill switch
- Explicitly not SRPT + enumerate failure modes

Lena Fischer: Ack. I’ll push an ADR revision today and tag reviewers. :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: Add me on the SRE sign-off for canary gating. Once metrics + dashboard spec are in, we can pre-approve the canary plan.

Allison Grant: Please loop me on metric names before you merge. I’ll sanity check against our conventions.

Hiro Tanaka: And loop kernels once you decide min-run/token budget defaults; we need to validate no kernel oscillation on A100/H100.

Noah Patel: Cool. Marking “changes requested” on ADR until the above is addressed.

Architecture Bot: Review status updated: changes requested by Noah Patel.

title: #partnerships: 1769990011
preview:
Lina Park: quick sync please 1 - final readiness for NimbusEdge + SI Pack marketplace launch. primary asks: sandbox for SI validation, press embargo rules, hero assets, SKU mapping, and day-0 support rota. target Wed 07:00 PT.
Jonah Reed: creative still needs badge SVG + 2 hero crops. asked Nimbus for source files. if late we fallback to neutral hero and hide partner badge.
Priya Shah: legal: we can do one early hands-on under NDA. will draft a single-use NDA addendum. need outlet name and tester emails.
Mateo Alvarez: ops: sandbox can be provisioned but need exact spec. proposed: 2x A10G-ish nodes, 60GB nvme, 8 vCPU, locked egress. is that ok?
Zoe Chen: marketing: embargo plan 1 - prefer Tue 10am PT embargo to bloggers, Wed 07:00 PT public. if outlet requests early hands-on we require NDA signed before access.
Samir Patel: SI lead here 1 - we need a 1-pager quickstart + a tiny runbook (3 bullets) and a contact email. I can draft quickstart tonight.
Lina Park: perfect. Samir also please include a 2-line troubleshooting FAQ for landing page.
Samir Patel: will do. also request a tiny "1-click deploy" snippet for the docs so SI can validate quickly.
Jonah Reed: creative asks for a 1s install GIF for the microsite hero slot. Nimbus said they can supply an MP4 and a static PNG fallback.
Priya Shah: NDA addendum will be ready in ~2h. who will collect signatures?
Lina Park: partnerships will coordinate signatures with Nimbus AM. Priya please drop the addendum link here when ready.
Mateo Alvarez: ops question: any objections to a temporary routing rule for first 48h to route inference-heavy requests to Dedicated pool (and fallback to quantized variant if latency > 350ms)? reduces cost blast risk.
Zoe Chen: agree. product + eng-platform should sign off but thumbs up from marketing.
Samir Patel: can we also surface a short note on the marketplace listing: "SI integration support hours: Wed-Fri 9-5 PT" and include the triage email? reduces confusion.
Lina Park: yes add that to the listing. Jonah can you reserve a CTA slot for "SI office hours"?
Jonah Reed: reserved. landing will have CTA + mini checklist: Install, Auth, Smoke-test.
eng-bot: doc created: https://redwood.docs/marketplace/nimbusedge-si-quickstart v0.9 (auto-tagged)
Priya Shah: NDA addendum uploaded: https://redwood.legal/nimbus-handson-addendum v1.0
Lina Park: timeline draft:```
- Mon AM: final images + SKU list in repo
- Mon noon: NDA signed for early hands-on (if used)
- Mon EOD: one-off early hands-on (NDA'd) if approved
- Tue AM: sandbox provisioned + SI smoke tests start
- Tue 17:00 PT: landing page staging cutover
- Wed 07:00 PT: blog + public announcement
- Wed 09:00 PT: marketplace listing live
- Thu 11:00 PT: first analytics + cost spike review
```
Mateo Alvarez: ops note: please confirm SKU mapping name convention so we don't mismatch marketplace IDs. send CSV with fields: sku_id, model_variant, region.
Samir Patel: SI will validate SKUs during sandbox smoke tests. will post results in this thread.
Zoe Chen: PR: if an outlet asks for an embargo exception, PR wants full outlet name + justification. we only allow one NDA'd early hands-on for a single outlet.
Lina Park: Nimbus suggested "InfraToday" as the outlet for early hands-on. PR, proceed with NDA flow?
Zoe Chen: proceed. PR will handle outreach once NDA signed.
Jonah Reed: creative fallback: if Nimbus images miss the cutover we will use a neutral hero and add a subtle "partner badge coming" pill. prevents blocking listing.
Priya Shah: legal confirms addendum allows single-use access and prohibits screenshots until public release. good.
eng-bot: runbook updated: /playbooks/marketplace-nimbusedge-golive v3.0 (fallback routing added)
Mateo Alvarez: provisioning note: sandbox creds expire 48h after creation. who should own rotation and invites?
Samir Patel: SI lead will own invites and initial rotation. ops keep rotation automated after smoke tests.
Lina Park: final blocker check 1 - anything else?
Jonah Reed: ask creative: do we support localized landing (JP/FR) at launch or later? Nimbus requested JP.
Zoe Chen: recommend EN-only at launch and wave 2 localization week 2. communicate expectations to Nimbus.
Lina Park: agreed. I'll notify Nimbus AM and ask for final assets by Mon EOD.
calendar-bot: event created: NimbusEdge Marketplace Launch 1 Wed 07:00 PT (invitees: partnerships, PR, marketing, eng-platform)
Lina Park: thanks team. I'll post a single checklist link here after lunch and update playbook with the sandbox spec.

title: #eng-releases: 1769118802
preview:
Connor O'Brien: Requesting a temporary release freeze for serving runtime rollouts until perf-canary signals stabilize + we finish threshold tuning. We had 2 warning spikes yesterday + 1 near-page this AM that look like baseline drift / ingestion lag, not real perf.

Connor O'Brien: Proposal:
- Freeze: runtime + kernel rollouts to prod (hosted + dedicated) for 48h
- Allowed: Sev1/Sev2 fixes w/ explicit signoff from SRE + runtime TL
- Goal: reduce overlapping changes while we tune min-samples/dampening + region/SKU thresholds

Connor O'Brien: Context links:
- Incident thread: https://slack.redwood.example/archives/incidents/p1769034455
- Canary baseline drift discussion: https://slack.redwood.example/archives/eng-runtime/p1768604412
- Current rollout schedule sheet: https://drive.redwood.example/eng-releases/runtime-rollouts

Noah Patel: +1 from runtime. The canary regression evaluator is too twitchy in us-east right now. We’re pushing a config change today to bump min sample size and add a 15m hold before escalating warning->page.

Allison Grant: To be clear, are we freezing only the runtime deploy train, or also telemetry/schema changes? Some of the noise is from metrics pipeline latency, so freezing runtime alone might not help.

Connor O'Brien: Good q. My intent is specifically changes that can affect latency/tps (runtime build, kernels, scheduler config, fleet placement). Telemetry/schema changes can proceed but please avoid breaking dashboards/alerts.

Sean Gallagher: SRE vote: freeze is reasonable. Oncall got a bunch of low-signal noise this week and it’s hard to tell what’s real vs baseline wobble. We should also lock the canary target model list during the freeze.

Vanessa Ortiz: Fleet side: we have a planned GPU driver patch in eu-west tonight. That could move perf a bit. Do we want to pause that too? It’s “infra” but impacts canary.

Connor O'Brien: If it moves perf and canary can’t attribute yet, we should defer unless it’s urgent. Can you check if we can slip 48h?

Vanessa Ortiz: I can slip it to Friday. Not urgent.

Hiro Tanaka: Kernel team had a minor kernel selection tweak queued (auto-select fallback ordering). Not merged yet. Happy to hold.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduler change (batching heuristic) is in staging only; was tentatively going to prod Thu. We can pause. I’d prefer the freeze includes scheduler flags too (we’ve seen queueing-time shift look like latency regression).

Logan Wright: Re: telemetry pipeline, we’re debugging ingestion delay in us-east. Worst-case 8–12m lag during peak. That made “observed window” mismatched vs baseline. We have a fix behind a feature flag and can roll it safely (it’s just buffering/flush). Not performance-impacting for inference.

Allison Grant: +1 to allow telemetry fixes. But we should call out: any telemetry change needs to keep label cardinality stable. Last week we almost exploded series count.

Rafael Mendes: From paging hygiene side: thank you. The near-page this AM was painful—looked like p95 +18% on a top model, but trace links were empty b/c metrics were late. Freeze helps us not chase ghosts.

Marissa Cole: Product perspective: ok with a 48h freeze as long as we keep the “break glass” path for real regressions. Also can we use this time to align what canary gates should block? Right now it’s unclear if warning blocks rollout or only paging.

Connor O'Brien: Agree. During freeze, I’ll draft a simple gating rule for Phase 1: warning does NOT auto-block, sustained paging DOES block + triggers rollback recommendation. We’ll iterate.

Talia Benson: Can someone state the exact start/end times and what’s included? I’ll update the release calendar + send a note.

Connor O'Brien: Start: now (post this thread). End: 48h from now (or earlier if Noah/Sean sign off that canary is stable). Included:
- Runtime build rollouts (any region/tier)
- Kernel changes / kernel selector behavior changes
- Scheduler config/flags that impact batching/queueing
- Fleet perf-impacting changes (driver, GPU SKU swap) unless urgent
Excluded:
- Telemetry pipeline fixes (w/ review)
- Console/dashboard updates

ReleaseBot: :rotating_light: Release freeze marker added to calendar: “Runtime Perf Canary Stabilization Freeze” (owner: Connor O'Brien).

Noah Patel: Plan for unfreeze criteria:
1) Min-samples + dampening config deployed
2) At least 24h without false positive warnings on the initial canary model set in us-east
3) Telemetry ingestion lag <2m p95 (Logan owns)

Sean Gallagher: +1. Also add: if we do get a real regression during freeze, we can still rollback/disable risky flags. Freeze is about forward rollouts.

Vanessa Ortiz: Confirmed: eu-west driver patch moved to Friday. I’ll note it on the infra change log.

Logan Wright: I’ll push telemetry lag fix today. Will post before/after graphs in #eng-platform.

Allison Grant: Please ensure canary alert payloads include “data freshness” / ingestion delay so oncall can quickly see when it’s a pipeline artifact.

Noah Patel: We can add a “metrics_lag_seconds” field to the incident-bot payload quickly (or at least in the runbook). I’ll sync with Caleb.

Mateo Alvarez: Approving freeze. Thanks for making this explicit—too many moving parts right now. Keep it tight (48h) and use it to get canary signal quality up.

Connor O'Brien: Thanks all. Action items:
- (Noah) deploy min-samples+dampening config today; lock canary model list for 48h
- (Logan) telemetry lag fix + report p95 lag
- (Vanessa) hold eu-west driver change until Friday
- (Connor) update release calendar + send note + draft gating rule for Phase 1

Talia Benson: I’ll send the note to eng-runtime/eng-sre/eng-platform and update the weekly release email. Please @me if anything should be exempted explicitly.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: Oncall will be grateful. If we still see noise after the config changes, we should consider lowering alert sensitivity or adding a longer confirmation window before paging.

Connor O'Brien: Ack. If we’re not stable by tomorrow afternoon, we’ll extend freeze or narrow paging scope to top-3 only until we tune per-region thresholds.
text:
Connor O'Brien: Requesting a temporary release freeze for serving runtime rollouts until perf-canary signals stabilize + we finish threshold tuning. We had 2 warning spikes yesterday + 1 near-page this AM that look like baseline drift / ingestion lag, not real perf.

Connor O'Brien: Proposal:
- Freeze: runtime + kernel rollouts to prod (hosted + dedicated) for 48h
- Allowed: Sev1/Sev2 fixes w/ explicit signoff from SRE + runtime TL
- Goal: reduce overlapping changes while we tune min-samples/dampening + region/SKU thresholds

Connor O'Brien: Context links:
- Incident thread: https://slack.redwood.example/archives/incidents/p1769034455
- Canary baseline drift discussion: https://slack.redwood.example/archives/eng-runtime/p1768604412
- Current rollout schedule sheet: https://drive.redwood.example/eng-releases/runtime-rollouts

Noah Patel: +1 from runtime. The canary regression evaluator is too twitchy in us-east right now. We’re pushing a config change today to bump min sample size and add a 15m hold before escalating warning->page.

Allison Grant: To be clear, are we freezing only the runtime deploy train, or also telemetry/schema changes? Some of the noise is from metrics pipeline latency, so freezing runtime alone might not help.

Connor O'Brien: Good q. My intent is specifically changes that can affect latency/tps (runtime build, kernels, scheduler config, fleet placement). Telemetry/schema changes can proceed but please avoid breaking dashboards/alerts.

Sean Gallagher: SRE vote: freeze is reasonable. Oncall got a bunch of low-signal noise this week and it’s hard to tell what’s real vs baseline wobble. We should also lock the canary target model list during the freeze.

Vanessa Ortiz: Fleet side: we have a planned GPU driver patch in eu-west tonight. That could move perf a bit. Do we want to pause that too? It’s “infra” but impacts canary.

Connor O'Brien: If it moves perf and canary can’t attribute yet, we should defer unless it’s urgent. Can you check if we can slip 48h?

Vanessa Ortiz: I can slip it to Friday. Not urgent.

Hiro Tanaka: Kernel team had a minor kernel selection tweak queued (auto-select fallback ordering). Not merged yet. Happy to hold.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduler change (batching heuristic) is in staging only; was tentatively going to prod Thu. We can pause. I’d prefer the freeze includes scheduler flags too (we’ve seen queueing-time shift look like latency regression).

Logan Wright: Re: telemetry pipeline, we’re debugging ingestion delay in us-east. Worst-case 8–12m lag during peak. That made “observed window” mismatched vs baseline. We have a fix behind a feature flag and can roll it safely (it’s just buffering/flush). Not performance-impacting for inference.

Allison Grant: +1 to allow telemetry fixes. But we should call out: any telemetry change needs to keep label cardinality stable. Last week we almost exploded series count.

Rafael Mendes: From paging hygiene side: thank you. The near-page this AM was painful—looked like p95 +18% on a top model, but trace links were empty b/c metrics were late. Freeze helps us not chase ghosts.

Marissa Cole: Product perspective: ok with a 48h freeze as long as we keep the “break glass” path for real regressions. Also can we use this time to align what canary gates should block? Right now it’s unclear if warning blocks rollout or only paging.

Connor O'Brien: Agree. During freeze, I’ll draft a simple gating rule for Phase 1: warning does NOT auto-block, sustained paging DOES block + triggers rollback recommendation. We’ll iterate.

Talia Benson: Can someone state the exact start/end times and what’s included? I’ll update the release calendar + send a note.

Connor O'Brien: Start: now (post this thread). End: 48h from now (or earlier if Noah/Sean sign off that canary is stable). Included:
- Runtime build rollouts (any region/tier)
- Kernel changes / kernel selector behavior changes
- Scheduler config/flags that impact batching/queueing
- Fleet perf-impacting changes (driver, GPU SKU swap) unless urgent
Excluded:
- Telemetry pipeline fixes (w/ review)
- Console/dashboard updates

ReleaseBot: :rotating_light: Release freeze marker added to calendar: “Runtime Perf Canary Stabilization Freeze” (owner: Connor O'Brien).

Noah Patel: Plan for unfreeze criteria:
1) Min-samples + dampening config deployed
2) At least 24h without false positive warnings on the initial canary model set in us-east
3) Telemetry ingestion lag <2m p95 (Logan owns)

Sean Gallagher: +1. Also add: if we do get a real regression during freeze, we can still rollback/disable risky flags. Freeze is about forward rollouts.

Vanessa Ortiz: Confirmed: eu-west driver patch moved to Friday. I’ll note it on the infra change log.

Logan Wright: I’ll push telemetry lag fix today. Will post before/after graphs in #eng-platform.

Allison Grant: Please ensure canary alert payloads include “data freshness” / ingestion delay so oncall can quickly see when it’s a pipeline artifact.

Noah Patel: We can add a “metrics_lag_seconds” field to the incident-bot payload quickly (or at least in the runbook). I’ll sync with Caleb.

Mateo Alvarez: Approving freeze. Thanks for making this explicit—too many moving parts right now. Keep it tight (48h) and use it to get canary signal quality up.

Connor O'Brien: Thanks all. Action items:
- (Noah) deploy min-samples+dampening config today; lock canary model list for 48h
- (Logan) telemetry lag fix + report p95 lag
- (Vanessa) hold eu-west driver change until Friday
- (Connor) update release calendar + send note + draft gating rule for Phase 1

Talia Benson: I’ll send the note to eng-runtime/eng-sre/eng-platform and update the weekly release email. Please @me if anything should be exempted explicitly.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: Oncall will be grateful. If we still see noise after the config changes, we should consider lowering alert sensitivity or adding a longer confirmation window before paging.

Connor O'Brien: Ack. If we’re not stable by tomorrow afternoon, we’ll extend freeze or narrow paging scope to top-3 only until we tune per-region thresholds.

title: #eng-sre: 1768821107
preview:
Aisha Bello: we should draft the statuspage comms playbook now, before we flip more traffic to tiered batching. what are the criteria for posting if latency worsens during rollout?

Rafael Mendes: +1. we’ve got abort criteria for canary, but statuspage criteria is separate (customer-visible). don’t want to over-post for a 1% canary blip.

Sean Gallagher: agreed. let’s define:
1) when we post
2) what we say (don’t overspec internal feature names)
3) who owns updates + cadence

Allison Grant: from metrics side, suggest we anchor on customer-visible SLOs and impact scope.
- Hosted API: fast tier p99 (streaming first-token) + standard tier p95 end-to-end are the ones folks feel
- also “time to first token” for streaming routes is super sensitive

Marissa Cole: also need a line on “if this is limited to canary cohort” -> we can do targeted comms (support + affected customers) instead of global statuspage.

Aisha Bello: ok propose triggers (hosted):
A) p99 TTFT for streaming routes in FAST tier > target by +25% for >= 15 min AND >5% traffic impacted
B) p95 end-to-end latency in STANDARD tier > target by +20% for >= 30 min AND >10% traffic impacted
C) any p99 > target by +50% for >= 10 min in 2+ regions
D) if we shed load / throttle (customer gets 429/503) due to overload mode as part of mitigation

Julian Moore: + include queueing signals? if we see queue depth runaway we’ll rollback fast, but that’s still “degraded performance” while happening.

Sean Gallagher: queue depth is internal; use it as investigation hint, not a public trigger. public triggers should be measurable externally.

Rafael Mendes: on traffic impacted: how do we compute? % of requests in component? e.g. /v1/chat completions only? or all inference?

Allison Grant: we can compute impact at two levels:
- request volume weighted across Hosted API inference endpoints
- additionally list “subset of endpoints” in message if only chat is affected
We already tag by route + model, but for statuspage keep it simple.

Aisha Bello: +1. impact scope on statuspage should read like “some customers in us-east-1 may see elevated latency for streaming responses”

Connor O'Brien: release train angle: during staged rollout we may have “intentional” changes (like cost-efficient tier enabling). still, if it degrades beyond SLO we should treat as incident.

Sean Gallagher: yes. rollout isn’t a free pass. criteria should be “customer experience degradation beyond published targets and not quickly self-resolving.”

Mateo Alvarez: (jumping in) strongly prefer we post if user-visible and persistent, even if we expect rollback soon. but avoid saying “new batching defaults broke x” publicly.

Marissa Cole: wording guidance:
- don’t mention “tiered batching” or internal flags
- do mention “we’re investigating elevated inference latency”
- if rollback executed: “we’ve reverted a recent change”
- include ETA only if real; otherwise “next update in 30 min”

Allison Grant: also define “post vs no-post” for canary:
- If canary is <2% traffic and ONLY affects canary cohort AND we can confirm isolation, no statuspage; notify support + internal #incidents and fix/rollback.
- If canary cohort includes paying customers broadly (not isolated) OR impact bleeds into non-canary, treat as normal incident -> statuspage.

Rafael Mendes: we should codify “isolation confirmation” requirements:
1) feature flag scope confirmed (flag service screenshot / config snapshot)
2) metrics show impacted requests have `tiered_defaults_enabled=true`
3) no correlated spike on non-enabled cohort

Aisha Bello: good. let’s also add “customer tickets” trigger: if Support gets 3+ independent reports in 20 min (same symptom/region), we post even if metrics are borderline.

SupportBot: FYI Support intake rule updated: tag “latency-regression” auto-escalates to #eng-sre-oncall when count >= 3 within 30m.

Sean Gallagher: nice. for thresholds, I’d tweak:
- FAST streaming TTFT p99: if > +30% for >= 10 min (fast tier is supposed to be strict)
- STANDARD p95: +20% for >= 30 min is ok
- COST-EFFICIENT: don’t page/statuspage based solely on that tier unless it impacts default traffic (since not default early)

Julian Moore: watch out: cost-efficient could become default later. playbook should be “current default tier(s) + any tier impacting >10% traffic.”

Allison Grant: can we add an explicit “error budget burn rate” trigger? e.g. 14-day SLO burn > 2x for 30 min.

Aisha Bello: for statuspage, burn rate is helpful internally but might delay posting. I’d keep as internal gate for paging severity.

Sean Gallagher: agree. statuspage trigger should be direct latency + scope + duration.

Rafael Mendes: what about partial region? we have region components on statuspage?

Marissa Cole: currently statuspage has “Hosted API” + “Console” + “Dedicated Control Plane” components. Not per-region. We can still mention region(s) in the body.

Connor O'Brien: update cadence:
- initial “Investigating” within 10 min of decision
- next update every 30 min until mitigated
- postmortem note not on statuspage, but we can link to incident summary if customer asks

Sean Gallagher: owner + approver:
- oncall SRE (me / primary) drafts + posts
- release manager (Rafael / Connor) provides context “rollback in progress”
- PM on-call (Marissa / Nikhil) sanity-check language if available, but don’t block if urgent

Aisha Bello: template v0:
1) Investigating: “We’re investigating elevated inference latency affecting some requests to the Hosted API. This may impact time-to-first-token and overall response times. Next update in 30 minutes.”
2) Identified/Mitigating: “We identified the cause and are rolling back a recent change / adjusting capacity. We’re monitoring recovery.”
3) Monitoring: “Latency is returning to normal levels. We’ll continue monitoring.”
4) Resolved: “This incident has been resolved. If you continue seeing issues, contact support.”

Allison Grant: add specific: mention 429/503 only if true. otherwise keep to latency.

Julian Moore: and avoid promising exact recovery time unless we’ve verified rollback ETA.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. also: if the issue is isolated to streaming endpoints, say that explicitly (customers doing non-streaming won’t panic).

Rafael Mendes: decision tree proposal:
- If impact >=10% traffic OR multiple customer reports OR 2+ regions AND duration >=10-15m -> statuspage
- Else if strictly canary + isolated -> no statuspage, internal + support-only
- If we trigger load shedding -> statuspage immediately

Sean Gallagher: sounds right. Aisha can you write this into the rollout checklist doc? I’ll add to oncall runbook as “Statuspage criteria - tiered defaults rollout.”

Aisha Bello: yep. I’ll post the criteria + templates in the rollout checklist and link here. also adding a checkbox: “statuspage decision made (Y/N) + rationale” for canary steps.

Marissa Cole: last note: for any statuspage post during rollout, also post a short internal blurb in #support and #customer-success so they can handle inbound.

Connor O'Brien: + we should pre-create the incident in statuspage for fast posting? (draft saved)

Sean Gallagher: do it. but keep it unpublished until needed. ok shipping this playbook.

Aisha Bello: cool, I’ll update with final thresholds:
- FAST streaming TTFT p99 > +30% for 10m AND >5% traffic
- STANDARD e2e p95 > +20% for 30m AND >10% traffic
- ANY tier p99 > +50% for 10m in 2+ regions OR load shedding -> immediate
- 3+ support reports in 20m -> immediate review for posting

Allison Grant: works. I’ll ensure dashboards have a “traffic impacted” panel to make this fast.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. linking this thread in the release brief too so it’s not tribal knowledge.

title: #eng-runtime: 1768793205
preview:
Sofia Ivanova: ok folks, final review thread for batching tier defaults before we cut runtime-1.22. Please sanity check the knob sets + call out remaining known issues we should document.

Sofia Ivanova: context: we reconciled the fast-tier p99 regression from last week + merged the cardinality hotfix. now we need to lock:
- defaults per tier (fast / standard / cost-efficient)
- any model-family constraints (70B vs 8B)
- overload behavior + guardrails

Sofia Ivanova: proposed FINAL values (decoder-only families; applies to Llama3.x, Mistral, Qwen2 unless noted)

FAST
- max_wait_ms: 2
- max_batch_tokens_total: 2048
- max_batch_requests: 8
- prefill_concurrency_limit: 2
- decode_step_tokens_cap: 256  (cap per tick to reduce long-decode hogging)
- admission_control: on
  - queue_soft_limit: 24
  - queue_hard_limit: 40 (reject/429)
- anti_starvation: on (age-based bump after 80ms)

STANDARD
- max_wait_ms: 6
- max_batch_tokens_total: 4096
- max_batch_requests: 16
- prefill_concurrency_limit: 3
- decode_step_tokens_cap: 512
- admission_control: on
  - queue_soft_limit: 64
  - queue_hard_limit: 120
- anti_starvation: on (age-based bump after 150ms)

COST-EFFICIENT
- max_wait_ms: 12
- max_batch_tokens_total: 8192
- max_batch_requests: 32
- prefill_concurrency_limit: 4
- decode_step_tokens_cap: 1024
- admission_control: on
  - queue_soft_limit: 128
  - queue_hard_limit: 240
- anti_starvation: on (age-based bump after 250ms)

Sofia Ivanova: 70B note: cap max_batch_tokens_total to 6144 for cost-efficient on H100-80G due to observed prefill spikes + KV pressure; on A100-80G cap to 4096.

Sofia Ivanova: streaming note: if request.streaming=true, enforce max_wait_ms = min(max_wait_ms, 4) for standard/cost-efficient. fast unchanged.

Sofia Ivanova: reason codes we will emit (per PR-28473):
- tier_default
- route_override
- tenant_override
- model_constraint
- overload_mode

Sofia Ivanova: remaining known issues I think we should carry:
1) mixed extremely-long prompts + short streaming in same queue: occasional jitter (still within p95 for standard, but p99 in fast can spike if we don’t shed)
2) very bursty traffic on cost-efficient: queue depth can oscillate; mitigated by hard limits but we should document it

Sofia Ivanova: pls reply with any “blocker” or “write it down as known limitation” by EOD.

Lena Fischer: :+1: these match what I have in the candidate sheet after the last rerun.

Lena Fischer: extra detail from the last benchmark pass (mixed workloads scenario v3):
- FAST: p99 improved after we lowered max_batch_tokens_total from 3072 -> 2048 + added decode_step_tokens_cap=256.
- STANDARD: stable; biggest win was prefill_concurrency_limit=3 (kept prefill from starving decode)
- COST-EFFICIENT: gains plateau after 8192 tokens; beyond that we got tail blowups under concurrency ramp

Lena Fischer: I’d like us to explicitly document the “streaming clamp” (min wait 4ms) in release notes. Otherwise folks will compare max_wait_ms=12 and assume we’re buffering streaming tokens for 12ms, which we aren’t.

Marcus Reed: perf suite results summary (topline; H100 hosted cluster, llama3.1-8b + llama3.1-70b):
- FAST tier: meets p95 target across both; p99 for 70b is still tight but within gate after admission control is enabled.
- STANDARD: meets p95 and p99 gates with margin
- COST-EFFICIENT: meets p95; p99 occasionally crosses gate on 70b when we allow >6k batch tokens

Marcus Reed: so I’m aligned w/ the 70B caps Sofia called out.

Marcus Reed: I also want to note: “max_batch_requests” matters more than we thought for short prompts. If we leave requests uncapped, batcher packs too many small reqs and decode gets spiky. The 8/16/32 caps fixed that without costing much throughput.

Omar El-Sayed: on fast tier: I’m good with max_wait_ms=2 + tight token caps.

Omar El-Sayed: one nuance: decode_step_tokens_cap=256 is doing a lot of work for tail. but it can reduce throughput for long outputs (obviously) and cost-efficient customers will complain if they accidentally land in fast.

Omar El-Sayed: so I’d like to ensure:
- default tier selection stays STANDARD unless explicitly configured
- overload_mode behavior for FAST is “shed earlier” not “increase batching”

Omar El-Sayed: known issue to add: if a tenant sets FAST on a route that routinely emits 2k+ output tokens, they’ll see throughput cliffs. that’s expected but we should state it.

Julian Moore: +1 on documenting overload behavior.

Julian Moore: current logic in PR-28480 branch is:
- FAST: overload_mode triggers at queue_soft_limit; actions: (1) turn on strict admission, (2) start returning 429 for new reqs once hard limit hit. we DO NOT increase wait.
- STANDARD/COST-EFFICIENT: overload_mode triggers at 1.2x queue_soft_limit; actions: (1) clamp max_batch_tokens_total down one “step” (e.g., 8192->6144) to reduce queue growth, (2) clamp max_wait_ms to 8 in cost-efficient.

Julian Moore: the second point might look counterintuitive (“cost-efficient clamps wait down”), but it prevents queue runaway and improves overall completion rate. we should mention it somewhere or we’ll get questions.

Sean Gallagher: SRE take: defaults look sane.

Sean Gallagher: for fast tier p99: please confirm the gating we’re using in canary is *per-region* not global aggregated. last time we got burned by a noisy region hiding in the mean.

Marcus Reed: yep, per-region gates are on in perf-canary PR-489. failures trigger on worst region, not avg.

Sean Gallagher: great.

Sean Gallagher: known issues list: can we add a bullet about “under overload, fast-tier will shed; customers may see more 429s rather than latency inflation”? That’s the right behavior but support will need to know.

Allison Grant: observability note: with tier labels re-enabled post-hotfix (pr-28544), we’re safe on cardinality if:
- tier is only one of {fast, standard, cost_efficient}
- we do NOT tag reason_code as a metric label on high-QPS histograms (it remains an event + sampled trace attribute)

Allison Grant: please don’t let anyone add `reason_code` to `latency_ms_bucket{...}` later “for convenience”. :)

Lena Fischer: re model-family constraints: we should call out qwen2-72b behaves like llama70b (same caps). mistral-large is even more sensitive (prefill spikes). I’d propose: any model with hidden_size>=8192 gets the 70B caps by default.

Sofia Ivanova: agree. we can implement that in the catalog constraint mapping (Quentin already has the `batching_profile_class` field). for the doc, we’ll phrase it as “large models may apply tighter batch-token caps automatically.”

Rina Kobayashi: benchmark lab note: the published profiles should include both “base” and “large-model constrained” variants. Otherwise external folks will try to reproduce with 70B and wonder why their numbers don’t match.

Rina Kobayashi: I can update the profile publication page to show:
- fast/base
- standard/base
- cost-efficient/base
- cost-efficient/large-model-cap
and footnote the auto-constraint.

Jonas Weber: evals/quality: no regressions correlated with these batching changes in the promptset runs. biggest “difference” reports we saw were actually truncation from customer max_tokens settings.

Jonas Weber: but I want one known issue: cost-efficient + aggressive batching can increase time-to-first-token for streaming when customers send long prompts. the “streaming clamp” helps, but TTFT still shifts upward vs fast. expected tradeoff; we should be explicit.

Omar El-Sayed: +1; TTFT vs throughput tradeoff is literally the whole tier story.

Marcus Reed: small clarification: streaming clamp currently is `if streaming then max_wait_ms = min(max_wait_ms, 4)` for standard and cost-efficient. In practice, fast already at 2.

Marcus Reed: with that, streaming TTFT delta for cost-efficient was ~+35-60ms p50 vs standard in the mixed scenario. (still within standard tier target for TTFT, but customers expecting “instant” should use fast.)

Sofia Ivanova: capturing known issues / limitations to include in release notes + docs:
- FAST is not intended for long generations; throughput cliffs are expected for high output-token routes
- Under overload, FAST sheds (429) rather than inflating latency
- For large models (hidden_size>=8192), cost-efficient may apply tighter max_batch_tokens_total caps automatically
- Streaming TTFT is higher in cost-efficient vs standard/fast; clamp prevents worst-case buffering but can’t remove tradeoff
- Mixed extreme-long prefill + short streaming can still create jitter; mitigated by anti-starvation + decode cap

Sofia Ivanova: anything else before we lock?

Allison Grant: last ask: ensure the dashboard panels show effective tier + whether large-model constraint was applied. otherwise debugging becomes impossible.

Julian Moore: that’s in the reason codes: `model_constraint` emitted when large-model cap triggers. Console can show “Effective profile: cost-efficient (large-model cap)” with that.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE, this is good to ship.

Mateo Alvarez: I’m good with these final defaults.

Mateo Alvarez: Sofia pls make sure the release notes call out the overload behavior + the fact we’re defaulting to STANDARD unless the route policy opts into fast/cost-efficient. That’s the main expectation-setting.

Mateo Alvarez: once that’s in, consider this sign-off for runtime-1.22 cut. :thumbsup:

Sofia Ivanova: ack. locking values as proposed. I’ll update the release notes doc + ping Connor for train inclusion. thanks all.
text:
Sofia Ivanova: ok folks, final review thread for batching tier defaults before we cut runtime-1.22. Please sanity check the knob sets + call out remaining known issues we should document.

Sofia Ivanova: context: we reconciled the fast-tier p99 regression from last week + merged the cardinality hotfix. now we need to lock:
- defaults per tier (fast / standard / cost-efficient)
- any model-family constraints (70B vs 8B)
- overload behavior + guardrails

Sofia Ivanova: proposed FINAL values (decoder-only families; applies to Llama3.x, Mistral, Qwen2 unless noted)

FAST
- max_wait_ms: 2
- max_batch_tokens_total: 2048
- max_batch_requests: 8
- prefill_concurrency_limit: 2
- decode_step_tokens_cap: 256  (cap per tick to reduce long-decode hogging)
- admission_control: on
  - queue_soft_limit: 24
  - queue_hard_limit: 40 (reject/429)
- anti_starvation: on (age-based bump after 80ms)

STANDARD
- max_wait_ms: 6
- max_batch_tokens_total: 4096
- max_batch_requests: 16
- prefill_concurrency_limit: 3
- decode_step_tokens_cap: 512
- admission_control: on
  - queue_soft_limit: 64
  - queue_hard_limit: 120
- anti_starvation: on (age-based bump after 150ms)

COST-EFFICIENT
- max_wait_ms: 12
- max_batch_tokens_total: 8192
- max_batch_requests: 32
- prefill_concurrency_limit: 4
- decode_step_tokens_cap: 1024
- admission_control: on
  - queue_soft_limit: 128
  - queue_hard_limit: 240
- anti_starvation: on (age-based bump after 250ms)

Sofia Ivanova: 70B note: cap max_batch_tokens_total to 6144 for cost-efficient on H100-80G due to observed prefill spikes + KV pressure; on A100-80G cap to 4096.

Sofia Ivanova: streaming note: if request.streaming=true, enforce max_wait_ms = min(max_wait_ms, 4) for standard/cost-efficient. fast unchanged.

Sofia Ivanova: reason codes we will emit (per PR-28473):
- tier_default
- route_override
- tenant_override
- model_constraint
- overload_mode

Sofia Ivanova: remaining known issues I think we should carry:
1) mixed extremely-long prompts + short streaming in same queue: occasional jitter (still within p95 for standard, but p99 in fast can spike if we don’t shed)
2) very bursty traffic on cost-efficient: queue depth can oscillate; mitigated by hard limits but we should document it

Sofia Ivanova: pls reply with any “blocker” or “write it down as known limitation” by EOD.

Lena Fischer: :+1: these match what I have in the candidate sheet after the last rerun.

Lena Fischer: extra detail from the last benchmark pass (mixed workloads scenario v3):
- FAST: p99 improved after we lowered max_batch_tokens_total from 3072 -> 2048 + added decode_step_tokens_cap=256.
- STANDARD: stable; biggest win was prefill_concurrency_limit=3 (kept prefill from starving decode)
- COST-EFFICIENT: gains plateau after 8192 tokens; beyond that we got tail blowups under concurrency ramp

Lena Fischer: I’d like us to explicitly document the “streaming clamp” (min wait 4ms) in release notes. Otherwise folks will compare max_wait_ms=12 and assume we’re buffering streaming tokens for 12ms, which we aren’t.

Marcus Reed: perf suite results summary (topline; H100 hosted cluster, llama3.1-8b + llama3.1-70b):
- FAST tier: meets p95 target across both; p99 for 70b is still tight but within gate after admission control is enabled.
- STANDARD: meets p95 and p99 gates with margin
- COST-EFFICIENT: meets p95; p99 occasionally crosses gate on 70b when we allow >6k batch tokens

Marcus Reed: so I’m aligned w/ the 70B caps Sofia called out.

Marcus Reed: I also want to note: “max_batch_requests” matters more than we thought for short prompts. If we leave requests uncapped, batcher packs too many small reqs and decode gets spiky. The 8/16/32 caps fixed that without costing much throughput.

Omar El-Sayed: on fast tier: I’m good with max_wait_ms=2 + tight token caps.

Omar El-Sayed: one nuance: decode_step_tokens_cap=256 is doing a lot of work for tail. but it can reduce throughput for long outputs (obviously) and cost-efficient customers will complain if they accidentally land in fast.

Omar El-Sayed: so I’d like to ensure:
- default tier selection stays STANDARD unless explicitly configured
- overload_mode behavior for FAST is “shed earlier” not “increase batching”

Omar El-Sayed: known issue to add: if a tenant sets FAST on a route that routinely emits 2k+ output tokens, they’ll see throughput cliffs. that’s expected but we should state it.

Julian Moore: +1 on documenting overload behavior.

Julian Moore: current logic in PR-28480 branch is:
- FAST: overload_mode triggers at queue_soft_limit; actions: (1) turn on strict admission, (2) start returning 429 for new reqs once hard limit hit. we DO NOT increase wait.
- STANDARD/COST-EFFICIENT: overload_mode triggers at 1.2x queue_soft_limit; actions: (1) clamp max_batch_tokens_total down one “step” (e.g., 8192->6144) to reduce queue growth, (2) clamp max_wait_ms to 8 in cost-efficient.

Julian Moore: the second point might look counterintuitive (“cost-efficient clamps wait down”), but it prevents queue runaway and improves overall completion rate. we should mention it somewhere or we’ll get questions.

Sean Gallagher: SRE take: defaults look sane.

Sean Gallagher: for fast tier p99: please confirm the gating we’re using in canary is *per-region* not global aggregated. last time we got burned by a noisy region hiding in the mean.

Marcus Reed: yep, per-region gates are on in perf-canary PR-489. failures trigger on worst region, not avg.

Sean Gallagher: great.

Sean Gallagher: known issues list: can we add a bullet about “under overload, fast-tier will shed; customers may see more 429s rather than latency inflation”? That’s the right behavior but support will need to know.

Allison Grant: observability note: with tier labels re-enabled post-hotfix (pr-28544), we’re safe on cardinality if:
- tier is only one of {fast, standard, cost_efficient}
- we do NOT tag reason_code as a metric label on high-QPS histograms (it remains an event + sampled trace attribute)

Allison Grant: please don’t let anyone add `reason_code` to `latency_ms_bucket{...}` later “for convenience”. :)

Lena Fischer: re model-family constraints: we should call out qwen2-72b behaves like llama70b (same caps). mistral-large is even more sensitive (prefill spikes). I’d propose: any model with hidden_size>=8192 gets the 70B caps by default.

Sofia Ivanova: agree. we can implement that in the catalog constraint mapping (Quentin already has the `batching_profile_class` field). for the doc, we’ll phrase it as “large models may apply tighter batch-token caps automatically.”

Rina Kobayashi: benchmark lab note: the published profiles should include both “base” and “large-model constrained” variants. Otherwise external folks will try to reproduce with 70B and wonder why their numbers don’t match.

Rina Kobayashi: I can update the profile publication page to show:
- fast/base
- standard/base
- cost-efficient/base
- cost-efficient/large-model-cap
and footnote the auto-constraint.

Jonas Weber: evals/quality: no regressions correlated with these batching changes in the promptset runs. biggest “difference” reports we saw were actually truncation from customer max_tokens settings.

Jonas Weber: but I want one known issue: cost-efficient + aggressive batching can increase time-to-first-token for streaming when customers send long prompts. the “streaming clamp” helps, but TTFT still shifts upward vs fast. expected tradeoff; we should be explicit.

Omar El-Sayed: +1; TTFT vs throughput tradeoff is literally the whole tier story.

Marcus Reed: small clarification: streaming clamp currently is `if streaming then max_wait_ms = min(max_wait_ms, 4)` for standard and cost-efficient. In practice, fast already at 2.

Marcus Reed: with that, streaming TTFT delta for cost-efficient was ~+35-60ms p50 vs standard in the mixed scenario. (still within standard tier target for TTFT, but customers expecting “instant” should use fast.)

Sofia Ivanova: capturing known issues / limitations to include in release notes + docs:
- FAST is not intended for long generations; throughput cliffs are expected for high output-token routes
- Under overload, FAST sheds (429) rather than inflating latency
- For large models (hidden_size>=8192), cost-efficient may apply tighter max_batch_tokens_total caps automatically
- Streaming TTFT is higher in cost-efficient vs standard/fast; clamp prevents worst-case buffering but can’t remove tradeoff
- Mixed extreme-long prefill + short streaming can still create jitter; mitigated by anti-starvation + decode cap

Sofia Ivanova: anything else before we lock?

Allison Grant: last ask: ensure the dashboard panels show effective tier + whether large-model constraint was applied. otherwise debugging becomes impossible.

Julian Moore: that’s in the reason codes: `model_constraint` emitted when large-model cap triggers. Console can show “Effective profile: cost-efficient (large-model cap)” with that.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE, this is good to ship.

Mateo Alvarez: I’m good with these final defaults.

Mateo Alvarez: Sofia pls make sure the release notes call out the overload behavior + the fact we’re defaulting to STANDARD unless the route policy opts into fast/cost-efficient. That’s the main expectation-setting.

Mateo Alvarez: once that’s in, consider this sign-off for runtime-1.22 cut. :thumbsup:

Sofia Ivanova: ack. locking values as proposed. I’ll update the release notes doc + ping Connor for train inclusion. thanks all.

title: #eng-sre: 1768526344
preview:
Rafael Mendes: We need to settle anti-flap + dedupe for perf-canary alerts before we turn on paging. Current incident-bot handler is basically “one Slack thread per firing alertname”, which is… not enough.

Allison Grant: +1. We’re going to get spam if p95 oscillates around threshold. Need: (1) alert rule dampening, (2) incident-bot dedupe key, (3) clear “resolved” behavior.

Aisha Bello: For alerting side: what are we thinking for minimum samples? If the canary is low QPS for overhead reasons, we can’t do 1-min windows.

Nadia Rahman: FWIW telemetry pipeline produces stable histograms at 1m resolution but variance is high under 50 req/min, especially for p95/p99. I’d strongly prefer the evaluator enforce a min sample size gate.

Sean Gallagher: From on-call perspective: fewer pages > faster pages. I’m ok delaying page by 10–15m if it reduces false positives.

Logan Wright: Dedupe key proposal: incident-bot should dedupe on a normalized “incident identity” that doesn’t include the volatile labels (baseline hash, run_id, pod). Something like:

Logan Wright: 
```
dedupe_key = (
  service="perf-canary",
  metric={latency_p95|tps_drop|queueing_p95},
  model,
  region,
  tier,
  severity
)
```

Logan Wright: optionally include hardware_sku but only if we *actually* route by SKU.

Allison Grant: Please don’t include runtime_build_id in dedupe key. That’ll explode threads during a rollout where we see both builds.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. But we still need the alert payload to show runtime_build_id + kernel tag for triage.

Caleb Johnson: +1 payload fields are fine. For dedupe, you want “same customer impact surface”. That’s model+region+tier+metric.

Aisha Bello: On anti-flap: can we do “X out of Y evaluations” gating? Example: p95 regression condition true for 3 out of last 5 minutes, AND min_samples >= N.

Nadia Rahman: Yes, evaluator already has per-window sample counts. Suggest min_samples per evaluation window AND across the combined dampening window.

Sean Gallagher: What numbers? If we set min_samples too high, we’ll never alert on lower-traffic regions.

Mateo Alvarez: For phase 1 (us-east), canary QPS should be enough. We can tune region-by-region later. But we need sane defaults now.

Allison Grant: Proposed default for warning:
- window: 10m rolling
- condition: regression >= threshold for 2 consecutive evals (each eval 5m)
- min_samples: >= 200 requests total over 10m for that model/region/tier
- hold-down: once fired, don’t re-fire for 30m unless severity escalates

Allison Grant: Paging:
- window: 15m rolling
- condition: 3 consecutive evals (5m each)
- min_samples: >= 400 requests total
- hold-down: 60m

Rafael Mendes: That hold-down is for *new* incidents right? We still want updates posted in the existing thread.

Caleb Johnson: incident-bot can do: if dedupe_key exists and status=firing, post “still firing” every 15m max (rate-limit updates). And on resolved, post a resolution note.

Logan Wright: We should also set a max update frequency per dedupe_key. E.g. no more than 1 message / 10m per incident, unless severity changes.

Aisha Bello: Severity change handling: warning -> page should post in same thread but also page the on-call channel. Dedupe key includes severity right now, which would create a new thread.

Allison Grant: Good catch. Dedupe should not include severity then. Severity should be a field that can change within the same incident.

Logan Wright: Ok updated:

Logan Wright: 
```
dedupe_key = (
  service="perf-canary",
  metric,
  model,
  region,
  tier
)

thread_action:
- if new dedupe_key: create thread
- if existing: update thread
- if severity escalates: @pager + add "ESCALATED" line, keep same thread
```

Sean Gallagher: That works for me. One thread = one “thing broken”. If it gets worse, same thread.

Nadia Rahman: On min_samples: define “request” consistently. A canary run can be streaming; do we count per request or per token?

Mateo Alvarez: For regression definition we’re using request-level histograms (latency per request) and aggregate tokens/sec. So min_samples should be request count for latency metrics. For tokens/sec, maybe min_samples is “total generated tokens” to reduce noise.

Aisha Bello: +1. tokens/sec can swing with small token totals.

Allison Grant: Suggest:
- latency metrics: min_requests >= 200 (warn), >= 400 (page)
- tokens/sec metric: min_tokens >= 200k (warn), >= 400k (page)

Nadia Rahman: 200k tokens in 10m might be high for some models if we’re limiting overhead.

Rafael Mendes: Let’s not block on perfect numbers. Can we set tokens/sec min gate as “min_requests >= 100 AND min_tokens >= 50k” for warn, then revisit?

Sean Gallagher: Works. And we can disable paging for tokens/sec initially, only warn.

Allison Grant: Yes: page only on p95 latency regressions for top models in phase 1. tokens/sec stays warn-only until we have a week of data.

Caleb Johnson: Dedupe edge case: region outage causes canary timeouts, we might fire both “latency_p95 regression” and “canary_error_rate high”. Those should be separate dedupe_keys, but same root cause.

Rafael Mendes: That’s fine. On-call can correlate. Also, canary_error_rate should route to SRE but not page runtime.

Allison Grant: Routing matrix reminder:
- perf regression (latency/tps): #eng-runtime + oncall-runtime when paging
- canary health (errors/timeouts): #eng-sre first

incident-bot: (system) Noted: update planned for dedupe_key to exclude severity; rate-limit updates; resolution message enabled. (reactions: :white_check_mark:)

Logan Wright: I’ll update incident-bot handler accordingly. Need final list of fields in payload + link templates (Grafana dashboard + runbook).

Aisha Bello: I’ll update alert rule drafts: add min sample gate, X-of-Y evaluations, hold-down, and warn-only for tokens/sec. Will post PR for review.

Sean Gallagher: Please include in the bot message a single-line summary:
“model=…, region=…, tier=…, metric=…, observed=…, baseline=…, delta=…, samples=…, runtime_build=…, kernel=…”
The first message should be enough to decide if it’s real.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also add “next steps” line with runbook link and who to page if kernel tag changed.

Allison Grant: Cool. Action items:
- Logan: incident-bot dedupe + update throttling + severity escalation in same thread
- Aisha: alert policy updates (min samples, X-of-Y, warn/page split)
- Nadia: sanity-check sample gates vs expected canary QPS (us-east)

Mateo Alvarez: Thanks. Let’s keep it conservative. I’d rather miss one mild regression than page the world for noise.

title: #eng-sre: 1768438820
preview:
Aisha Bello: Kicking off severity policy for perf-canary alerts. Need agreement on: what’s WARN vs PAGE, and who gets paged when a top-model regresses. Goal is to avoid paging on noise but still catch real tail latency regressions fast.

Aisha Bello: Proposal v0:
- WARN: p95 e2e latency +8% vs baseline sustained 10m (min N=200) OR tokens/sec -8% sustained 10m (min N=200)
- PAGE (Sev2): p95 +15% sustained 15m (min N=500) OR tokens/sec -15% sustained 15m (min N=500)
- PAGE (Sev1): p95 +25% sustained 15m (min N=500) AND affects >=2 regions OR top-model in us-east

Aisha Bello: Routing:
- WARN -> #eng-runtime + #eng-sre (no page)
- PAGE -> oncall (SRE primary) + runtime secondary in thread

Sean Gallagher: +1 on tiered, but I’d like paging to be *only* for top-models initially. For non-top models, page is going to kill us during tuning.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. If we page for any model regression we’ll page weekly (at least) during kernel/scheduler iteration.

Allison Grant: Can we define “top model” concretely as a list in control plane config? If it’s implicit (top by usage) it’ll churn and change paging behavior unexpectedly.

Noah Patel: Yeah, canary config has a curated set anyway. We can tag models with `priority=top|high-risk|standard` and only enable paging for `top` in phase 1.

Aisha Bello: Works for me. We can have a constant allowlist for paging.

Mateo Alvarez: From runtime side: I’m fine with paging only top-models. But I want WARN to trigger for all models in canary so we see issues early without waking people.

Paula Mendes: Product request: paging should be strict and explainable. Also we need a clear “what happens” when paging triggers (e.g. pause rollout gates). But we can separate that from this thread.

Logan Wright: Metric question: are we using p95 end-to-end or runtime-only? p95 e2e includes queueing + gateway stuff; might be noisy.

Aisha Bello: I think severity policy should key on e2e p95 for customer impact, but include runtime-only + queueing breakdown in alert payload for triage.

Noah Patel: +1. Canary will emit both and we can put both in the payload.

Sean Gallagher: Thresholds: +8% warn seems ok *if* we have anti-flap. For paging, +15% might still be noisy depending on model variance. Can we require 2 consecutive windows / burn-rate style check?

Aisha Bello: Yes. Proposed anti-flap:
- Evaluate in 5m windows.
- WARN requires 2/2 windows breaching (10m total).
- PAGE requires 3/3 windows breaching (15m total).
- Clear requires 2/2 windows healthy.

Rafael Mendes: Please also add “cooldown” after a page fires so incident-bot doesn’t re-page every eval cycle.

Aisha Bello: Yep: 60m cooldown for paging alerts, dedupe key = model+region+tier+signal.

Allison Grant: Re: min sample sizes. Is N=500 realistic for low-QPS models? For top models yes, but others may never hit that.

Noah Patel: For non-top models we’re not paging; min sample can be lower for WARN. Also canary QPS is under a budget; N depends on QPS and window. We can do per-priority min sample.

Aisha Bello: Updated proposal:
- WARN (all canary models): +10% p95 e2e OR -10% tokens/sec, sustained 10m, min N=150
- PAGE Sev2 (top models only): +20% p95 e2e OR -20% tokens/sec, sustained 15m, min N=400
- PAGE Sev1 (top models only): +30% p95 e2e sustained 15m, min N=400, AND observed in >=2 regions OR also shows in runtime-only p95 (to avoid paging on pure gateway blips)

Sean Gallagher: That feels safer. I like adding a “runtime-only corroboration” for Sev1.

Logan Wright: For that corroboration, make sure runtime-only histogram has same labels; otherwise joining in alerting gets ugly.

Aisha Bello: Noted. We’ll keep the alert rule simple: for Sev1, require `runtime_p95` > baseline by +25% in same window (in addition to e2e).

Rafael Mendes: Who gets paged?
- SRE oncall always
- Runtime oncall as secondary only for Sev1? Or always add them for Sev2 too?

Mateo Alvarez: Prefer: Sev2 pages SRE only, but auto-notify #eng-runtime with context. Sev1 pages both SRE and runtime oncall.

Noah Patel: +1. Runtime folks will jump in on Sev2 anyway if it’s real, but no need to wake two people for a Sev2 that might be infra.

Allison Grant: Can we codify this in the policy doc + make sure the incident-bot message includes ownership hints? Like “looks like queueing-time increased -> infra/scheduler”.

Aisha Bello: Yes. Alert payload should include: baseline vs observed, confidence (sample size), and quick attribution fields: queueing delta, batch size shift, kernel tag change, runtime build tag.

Sean Gallagher: Also: paging hours? We page 24/7 for Sev1, but for Sev2 can we do business hours only during tuning?

Rafael Mendes: Strong preference: Sev2 paging 24/7 only after we’ve had 1-2 weeks of clean WARN-only. For phase 1, let’s keep Sev2 as “ticket + slack notify”, not a page.

Aisha Bello: Ok, so initial rollout:
- WARN enabled immediately (all canary models)
- PAGE Sev2 disabled (or routed to non-paging channel) for week 1
- PAGE Sev1 enabled for top models only once we’ve validated baseline + cardinality

Mateo Alvarez: Fine. But put a date or criteria for enabling Sev1 so it doesn’t drift.

Aisha Bello: Criteria:
- Baselines computed for top models in us-east
- 0 alert storms in 72h
- Telemetry ingestion latency <2m p95
- Oncall runbook reviewed

Allison Grant: Add “dashboard links verified” too. Otherwise it’s a page with no working links.

Aisha Bello: Added.

Aisha Bello: Action items:
- I’ll update the alert policy draft with: top-model allowlist, new thresholds, and routing matrix.
- @Rafael Mendes can you confirm paging target (PagerDuty service) + cooldown/dedupe requirements for incident-bot?
- @Noah Patel please confirm we can emit runtime-only p95 with same label set (model, model_version, region, tier, hardware_sku) w/ cardinality guardrails.

Rafael Mendes: Yep, will drop PD service + escalation chain in the doc. Cooldown 60m is good; dedupe key as you said.

Noah Patel: Will confirm with Logan/Omar. Label set should be stable; we’ll avoid per-request IDs. Hardware SKU might be optional for alerting but helpful for drilldown.

Sean Gallagher: Thanks. Please keep the policy explicit: when in doubt, don’t page. We can always tighten once we trust the canary.

Aisha Bello: :thumbsup: I’ll post updated draft in this thread by EOD.
text:
Aisha Bello: Kicking off severity policy for perf-canary alerts. Need agreement on: what’s WARN vs PAGE, and who gets paged when a top-model regresses. Goal is to avoid paging on noise but still catch real tail latency regressions fast.

Aisha Bello: Proposal v0:
- WARN: p95 e2e latency +8% vs baseline sustained 10m (min N=200) OR tokens/sec -8% sustained 10m (min N=200)
- PAGE (Sev2): p95 +15% sustained 15m (min N=500) OR tokens/sec -15% sustained 15m (min N=500)
- PAGE (Sev1): p95 +25% sustained 15m (min N=500) AND affects >=2 regions OR top-model in us-east

Aisha Bello: Routing:
- WARN -> #eng-runtime + #eng-sre (no page)
- PAGE -> oncall (SRE primary) + runtime secondary in thread

Sean Gallagher: +1 on tiered, but I’d like paging to be *only* for top-models initially. For non-top models, page is going to kill us during tuning.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. If we page for any model regression we’ll page weekly (at least) during kernel/scheduler iteration.

Allison Grant: Can we define “top model” concretely as a list in control plane config? If it’s implicit (top by usage) it’ll churn and change paging behavior unexpectedly.

Noah Patel: Yeah, canary config has a curated set anyway. We can tag models with `priority=top|high-risk|standard` and only enable paging for `top` in phase 1.

Aisha Bello: Works for me. We can have a constant allowlist for paging.

Mateo Alvarez: From runtime side: I’m fine with paging only top-models. But I want WARN to trigger for all models in canary so we see issues early without waking people.

Paula Mendes: Product request: paging should be strict and explainable. Also we need a clear “what happens” when paging triggers (e.g. pause rollout gates). But we can separate that from this thread.

Logan Wright: Metric question: are we using p95 end-to-end or runtime-only? p95 e2e includes queueing + gateway stuff; might be noisy.

Aisha Bello: I think severity policy should key on e2e p95 for customer impact, but include runtime-only + queueing breakdown in alert payload for triage.

Noah Patel: +1. Canary will emit both and we can put both in the payload.

Sean Gallagher: Thresholds: +8% warn seems ok *if* we have anti-flap. For paging, +15% might still be noisy depending on model variance. Can we require 2 consecutive windows / burn-rate style check?

Aisha Bello: Yes. Proposed anti-flap:
- Evaluate in 5m windows.
- WARN requires 2/2 windows breaching (10m total).
- PAGE requires 3/3 windows breaching (15m total).
- Clear requires 2/2 windows healthy.

Rafael Mendes: Please also add “cooldown” after a page fires so incident-bot doesn’t re-page every eval cycle.

Aisha Bello: Yep: 60m cooldown for paging alerts, dedupe key = model+region+tier+signal.

Allison Grant: Re: min sample sizes. Is N=500 realistic for low-QPS models? For top models yes, but others may never hit that.

Noah Patel: For non-top models we’re not paging; min sample can be lower for WARN. Also canary QPS is under a budget; N depends on QPS and window. We can do per-priority min sample.

Aisha Bello: Updated proposal:
- WARN (all canary models): +10% p95 e2e OR -10% tokens/sec, sustained 10m, min N=150
- PAGE Sev2 (top models only): +20% p95 e2e OR -20% tokens/sec, sustained 15m, min N=400
- PAGE Sev1 (top models only): +30% p95 e2e sustained 15m, min N=400, AND observed in >=2 regions OR also shows in runtime-only p95 (to avoid paging on pure gateway blips)

Sean Gallagher: That feels safer. I like adding a “runtime-only corroboration” for Sev1.

Logan Wright: For that corroboration, make sure runtime-only histogram has same labels; otherwise joining in alerting gets ugly.

Aisha Bello: Noted. We’ll keep the alert rule simple: for Sev1, require `runtime_p95` > baseline by +25% in same window (in addition to e2e).

Rafael Mendes: Who gets paged?
- SRE oncall always
- Runtime oncall as secondary only for Sev1? Or always add them for Sev2 too?

Mateo Alvarez: Prefer: Sev2 pages SRE only, but auto-notify #eng-runtime with context. Sev1 pages both SRE and runtime oncall.

Noah Patel: +1. Runtime folks will jump in on Sev2 anyway if it’s real, but no need to wake two people for a Sev2 that might be infra.

Allison Grant: Can we codify this in the policy doc + make sure the incident-bot message includes ownership hints? Like “looks like queueing-time increased -> infra/scheduler”.

Aisha Bello: Yes. Alert payload should include: baseline vs observed, confidence (sample size), and quick attribution fields: queueing delta, batch size shift, kernel tag change, runtime build tag.

Sean Gallagher: Also: paging hours? We page 24/7 for Sev1, but for Sev2 can we do business hours only during tuning?

Rafael Mendes: Strong preference: Sev2 paging 24/7 only after we’ve had 1-2 weeks of clean WARN-only. For phase 1, let’s keep Sev2 as “ticket + slack notify”, not a page.

Aisha Bello: Ok, so initial rollout:

- PAGE Sev2 disabled (or routed to non-paging channel) for week 1
- PAGE Sev1 enabled for top models only once we’ve validated baseline + cardinality

Mateo Alvarez: Fine. But put a date or criteria for enabling Sev1 so it doesn’t drift.

Aisha Bello: Criteria:

- 0 alert storms in 72h
- Telemetry ingestion latency <2m p95
- Oncall runbook reviewed

Allison Grant: Add “dashboard links verified” too. Otherwise it’s a page with no working links.

Aisha Bello: Added.

Aisha Bello: Action items:
- I’ll update the alert policy draft with: top-model allowlist, new thresholds, and routing matrix.
- @Rafael Mendes can you confirm paging target (PagerDuty service) + cooldown/dedupe requirements for incident-bot?
- @Noah Patel please confirm we can emit runtime-only p95 with same label set (model, model_version, region, tier, hardware_sku) w/ cardinality guardrails.
