title: #eng-runtime: 1768429031
preview:
Sofia Ivanova: ok, need to land initial knob values for fast/standard/cost-efficient tiers this week. biggest open q: mixed prompt lengths + streaming. how are we preventing starvation if the queue is full of short prompts?

Sofia Ivanova: current proposal in the design doc is basically tier -> {max_wait_ms, max_batch_tokens, max_batch_reqs, prefill/decode split}. i’m worried it’s too hand-wavy.

Lena Fischer: pulled from the candidate sheet (w/ H100 80GB numbers). very rough but a starting point:
- fast: max_wait_ms=2, max_batch_reqs=8, max_batch_tokens=8192
- standard: max_wait_ms=6, max_batch_reqs=16, max_batch_tokens=16384
- cost-efficient: max_wait_ms=15, max_batch_reqs=32, max_batch_tokens=32768

Lena Fischer: but i think we should split prefill vs decode caps or we’ll get weird behavior on long prompts (prefill dominates the batch token budget and decode gets stuck behind it)

Omar El-Sayed: +1 on split. prefill and decode have different sensitivity.
- prefill: throughput is good, but it drives time-to-first-token.
- decode: tail latency blows up if we let decode microbatches get too fat.

Omar El-Sayed: i’d rather have:
fast: prefill_max_batch_tokens=4-6k, decode_max_batch_tokens=2-3k, max_wait_ms ~1-2
standard: prefill 12k, decode 4-6k, wait ~5-8
cost-eff: prefill 24k, decode 8-10k, wait ~12-20

Marcus Reed: the starvation issue i’ve seen in perf suite is different: long prompts (8k-16k prompt) get perpetually "not fitting" because there’s always a bunch of 200-600 token prompts available. scheduler keeps greedily packing small ones, long ones keep aging.

Marcus Reed: current heuristic is basically "best-fit packing" with a short wait. if we don’t enforce fairness, long prompts can wait 10s+ under burst.

Noah Patel: we need an explicit fairness mechanism, not just tuned batch caps.

Noah Patel: two options:
1) per-length buckets + weighted round robin
2) global priority = f(age) so old/large requests eventually win packing

Noah Patel: IMO do (2) at minimum. if request age > X ms, it should preempt packing even if it makes the batch less "optimal".

Julian Moore: also remember overload behavior: we already have backpressure hooks.
- fast tier should start rejecting earlier (admission control) rather than letting queue depth grow
- cost-efficient can tolerate more queueing but we need a hard ceiling (otherwise you get the “queue is the batch” anti-pattern)

Julian Moore: if we implement age-based priority, we should be careful: under overload it can make things worse (oldest = biggest prompt, then you’re stuck doing massive prefill while everything else waits). might be ok for standard/cost but fast tier should be ruthless.

Sofia Ivanova: can we make starvation protection tier-specific? e.g.
- fast: protect tail for short prompts/streaming, accept that long prompts might be slower or rejected
- standard: fairness across prompt lengths
- cost-efficient: fairness + bigger batches but hard queue delay cap

Lena Fischer: that seems aligned w/ customer messaging too. fast tier is “low latency”, not “handles 32k prompt under 500ms”.

Rina Kobayashi: from the benchmark side: we can gate on “no starvation” with a mixed workload scenario: 80% short prompts, 20% long prompts. pass criteria could be: long prompt p95 queue_delay < N (tier-specific). otherwise we’ll never see it until prod.

Marcus Reed: yup. perf suite change is easy, but we need the scheduler logic first.

Omar El-Sayed: on streaming: time-to-first-token is dominated by prefill. so for fast tier + streaming, i’d set max_wait_ms=0 or 1 (basically continuous batching but minimal waiting). you’ll still get some batching from co-arrivals.

Omar El-Sayed: for non-streaming, fast tier can wait 2ms and batch more; for streaming it should be lower. maybe max_wait_ms depends on streaming flag.

Noah Patel: careful: per-request wait budget can explode complexity. but we already have streaming flag in the request metadata, so conditional is fine.

Sofia Ivanova: ok so concretely, do we want to define knobs as:
- max_wait_ms_streaming
- max_wait_ms_non_streaming
- prefill_max_batch_tokens
- decode_max_batch_tokens
- max_batch_reqs
plus fairness/admission policies?

Julian Moore: + “queue_delay_cap_ms” for overload. when exceeded, shed or degrade. reason code needs to say why.

Lena Fischer: agree. minimal set that is explainable.

Mateo Alvarez: joining late — want to avoid default set that “looks right” but fails under real traffic. we need a simple story:
1) tiers map to specific SLOs
2) knobs are conservative for fast
3) we have explicit starvation protection

Mateo Alvarez: if we can’t explain why a long request waited, support will hate us.

Marcus Reed: on explainability: if we do length buckets, we can emit reason codes like:
- selected_bucket=short/med/long
- schedule_policy=rr/age_boost
- rejected_due_to=queue_delay_cap

Marcus Reed: and then we can show “long queue share throttled due to fast tier latency protection” vs “long request aged out”.

Noah Patel: ok, what’s the actual starvation fix?

Noah Patel: proposal:
- define length buckets by prompt_tokens: [0-512], (512-2048], (2048-8192], (8192+)
- each bucket has its own queue
- scheduler cycle uses weighted fair queueing
- if any request age > age_boost_ms, treat its bucket weight as +K until served

Noah Patel: for fast tier, weights heavily favor short buckets; for standard, flatter.

Omar El-Sayed: that works, but we need to ensure we don’t create head-of-line blocking inside the bucket. bucket queue should be ordered by arrival.

Lena Fischer: yep FIFO per bucket.

Julian Moore: how does this interact w/ packing? if you pick bucket A but the head request in A is too large to fit remaining tokens in the current batch, do you skip it or start a new batch?

Marcus Reed: skipping head breaks fairness (and can reintroduce starvation). i’d rather we:
- build the batch from scratch each dispatch
- choose one "anchor" request (from bucket selection)
- then fill remaining capacity greedily from other buckets w/ "fit" constraint

Marcus Reed: anchor ensures long requests get in.

Noah Patel: anchor concept is good. treat it like: pick anchor via WFQ/age, then pack.

Sofia Ivanova: ok, i like. so starvation solved by guaranteeing an anchor chosen by fairness.

Sofia Ivanova: then tier knobs are mostly about how big the batch can get + how long we wait before dispatch.

Lena Fischer: i’ll write down a concrete starting config so we have something to benchmark. propose (H100 baseline; we’ll re-tune for A10/L4 later):

Lena Fischer: 
```yaml
batching_tiers:
  fast:
    max_wait_ms_streaming: 1
    max_wait_ms_non_streaming: 2
    max_batch_reqs: 8
    prefill_max_batch_tokens: 6144
    decode_max_batch_tokens: 3072
    queue_delay_cap_ms: 50
    fairness:
      buckets: [512, 2048, 8192]
      weights: [6, 3, 1, 0.5]
      age_boost_ms: 30
  standard:
    max_wait_ms_streaming: 3
    max_wait_ms_non_streaming: 6
    max_batch_reqs: 16
    prefill_max_batch_tokens: 12288
    decode_max_batch_tokens: 6144
    queue_delay_cap_ms: 150
    fairness:
      buckets: [512, 2048, 8192]
      weights: [3, 2, 1, 1]
      age_boost_ms: 75
  cost_efficient:
    max_wait_ms_streaming: 6
    max_wait_ms_non_streaming: 15
    max_batch_reqs: 32
    prefill_max_batch_tokens: 24576
    decode_max_batch_tokens: 9216
    queue_delay_cap_ms: 300
    fairness:
      buckets: [512, 2048, 8192]
      weights: [2, 2, 1, 1]
      age_boost_ms: 120
```

Lena Fischer: numbers are starting points only. main idea: (a) split prefill/decode (b) streaming lower wait (c) anchor-based fairness.

Omar El-Sayed: decode_max_batch_tokens=9216 for cost-efficient feels high. decode tail might get spiky if we pack too much decode. can we cap decode lower and let prefill be the big lever?

Omar El-Sayed: i’d try 8192 or 6144 first, then increase if stable.

Marcus Reed: +1. decode tail is what customers notice even when they say “cost-efficient”.

Julian Moore: also queue_delay_cap_ms: cost-efficient 300ms is fine, but we need to clarify what happens at cap. reject? downgrade tier? shed only non-streaming? we should be explicit.

Sofia Ivanova: for v1: when cap hit -> return 429 w/ Retry-After + reason code `overload_queue_delay_cap`. no auto-downgrade across tiers (messaging nightmare).

Mateo Alvarez: agree, do not silently change tier. reject and make it obvious.

Rina Kobayashi: for benchmark gates, can we define pass/fail for queue_delay as well as end-to-end latency? otherwise cost-efficient will “pass” by just queueing.

Sofia Ivanova: yes. for each tier we’ll track queue_delay p95/p99 separately.

Noah Patel: one more corner: if we anchor a very large prefill (e.g., 16k prompt) in fast tier, that will destroy streaming TTFT for everything else that shares the GPU.

Noah Patel: so for fast tier fairness, maybe we should cap the max prompt length eligible? or treat 8192+ bucket weight as ~0 and only allow via age_boost if queue isn’t empty for too long.

Lena Fischer: i already made it 0.5 weight for 8192+ on fast. but still possible.

Julian Moore: can we also add an “anchor_max_prompt_tokens” per tier? fast might set to 8192. if request bigger, it doesn’t get to be anchor (still can be packed opportunistically if capacity allows).

Marcus Reed: that’s a nice guardrail. otherwise one giant prompt can anchor repeatedly when it gets old.

Sofia Ivanova: ok, add `anchor_max_prompt_tokens` to tier config. fast=8192, standard=16384, cost-efficient=inf for now.

Omar El-Sayed: and if request exceeds anchor cap, we should emit reason code `anchor_ineligible_prompt_too_large` so folks understand.

Mateo Alvarez: keep it tight. also make sure we’re not overfitting to H100. we need a plan for A10/L4 where prefill costs are different.

Rina Kobayashi: i can run quick sweeps on A10 once we have the config knob split. but we need the schema first.

Sofia Ivanova: action items:
- Lena: update proposal w/ (1) split prefill/decode (2) tier-specific streaming wait budgets (3) anchor_max_prompt_tokens
- Marcus/Noah: spec anchor-based WFQ logic (pseudo-code) and call out failure modes
- Julian: define overload behavior when queue_delay_cap hit + admission control recommendations for fast
- Rina: update perf suite plan: mixed length + streaming scenario + queue_delay gates

Marcus Reed: :thumbsup:

Omar El-Sayed: :+1: i’ll comment on the decode caps once we see first sweep.

Julian Moore: also pls remember metrics: we’ll want histograms for batch_size_tokens (prefill vs decode) + queue_delay by bucket. otherwise we can’t debug.

Sofia Ivanova: noted. i’ll ping Logan/Nadia separately re: keeping cardinality sane.

Mateo Alvarez: thanks. let’s not ship defaults that can silently starve traffic. anchor+caps feels like the right baseline.

Sofia Ivanova: ok closing the loop tomorrow after Lena posts updated config + Marcus posts pseudo-code.

```yaml
    max_wait_ms_non_streaming: 6
    max_batch_reqs: 16
    prefill_max_batch_tokens: 12288
    decode_max_batch_tokens: 6144
    queue_delay_cap_ms: 150
    fairness:
      buckets: [512, 2048, 8192]
      weights: [3, 2, 1, 1]
      age_boost_ms: 75
  cost_efficient:
    max_wait_ms_streaming: 6
    max_wait_ms_non_streaming: 15
    max_batch_reqs: 32
    prefill_max_batch_tokens: 24576
    decode_max_batch_tokens: 9216
    queue_delay_cap_ms: 300
    fairness:
      buckets: [512, 2048, 8192]
      weights: [2, 2, 1, 1]
      age_boost_ms: 120
```

Lena Fischer: numbers are starting points only. main idea: (a) split prefill/decode (b) streaming lower wait (c) anchor-based fairness.

Omar El-Sayed: decode_max_batch_tokens=9216 for cost-efficient feels high. decode tail might get spiky if we pack too much decode. can we cap decode lower and let prefill be the big lever?

Omar El-Sayed: i’d try 8192 or 6144 first, then increase if stable.

Marcus Reed: +1. decode tail is what customers notice even when they say “cost-efficient”.

Julian Moore: also queue_delay_cap_ms: cost-efficient 300ms is fine, but we need to clarify what happens at cap. reject? downgrade tier? shed only non-streaming? we should be explicit.

Sofia Ivanova: for v1: when cap hit -> return 429 w/ Retry-After + reason code `overload_queue_delay_cap`. no auto-downgrade across tiers (messaging nightmare).

Mateo Alvarez: agree, do not silently change tier. reject and make it obvious.

Rina Kobayashi: for benchmark gates, can we define pass/fail for queue_delay as well as end-to-end latency? otherwise cost-efficient will “pass” by just queueing.

Sofia Ivanova: yes. for each tier we’ll track queue_delay p95/p99 separately.

Noah Patel: one more corner: if we anchor a very large prefill (e.g., 16k prompt) in fast tier, that will destroy streaming TTFT for everything else that shares the GPU.

Noah Patel: so for fast tier fairness, maybe we should cap the max prompt length eligible? or treat 8192+ bucket weight as ~0 and only allow via age_boost if queue isn’t empty for too long.

Lena Fischer: i already made it 0.5 weight for 8192+ on fast. but still possible.

Julian Moore: can we also add an “anchor_max_prompt_tokens” per tier? fast might set to 8192. if request bigger, it doesn’t get to be anchor (still can be packed opportunistically if capacity allows).

Marcus Reed: that’s a nice guardrail. otherwise one giant prompt can anchor repeatedly when it gets old.

Sofia Ivanova: ok, add `anchor_max_prompt_tokens` to tier config. fast=8192, standard=16384, cost-efficient=inf for now.

Omar El-Sayed: and if request exceeds anchor cap, we should emit reason code `anchor_ineligible_prompt_too_large` so folks understand.

Mateo Alvarez: keep it tight. also make sure we’re not overfitting to H100. we need a plan for A10/L4 where prefill costs are different.

Rina Kobayashi: i can run quick sweeps on A10 once we have the config knob split. but we need the schema first.

Sofia Ivanova: action items:
- Lena: update proposal w/ (1) split prefill/decode (2) tier-specific streaming wait budgets (3) anchor_max_prompt_tokens
- Marcus/Noah: spec anchor-based WFQ logic (pseudo-code) and call out failure modes
- Julian: define overload behavior when queue_delay_cap hit + admission control recommendations for fast
- Rina: update perf suite plan: mixed length + streaming scenario + queue_delay gates

Marcus Reed: :thumbsup:

Omar El-Sayed: :+1: i’ll comment on the decode caps once we see first sweep.

Julian Moore: also pls remember metrics: we’ll want histograms for batch_size_tokens (prefill vs decode) + queue_delay by bucket. otherwise we can’t debug.

Sofia Ivanova: noted. i’ll ping Logan/Nadia separately re: keeping cardinality sane.

Mateo Alvarez: thanks. let’s not ship defaults that can silently starve traffic. anchor+caps feels like the right baseline.

Sofia Ivanova: ok closing the loop tomorrow after Lena posts updated config + Marcus posts pseudo-code.

title: #incidents: 1767735109
preview:
IncidentBot: :rotating_light: INCIDENT DECLARED (SEV-2) — Hosted API elevated tail latency
Scope: hosted/us-east
Start: 1767735109
Trigger: p99 latency > SLO for 10m (chat/generate)
Suspected: runtime-1.22 canary pool (kv compaction enabled)
Links: Grafana p99 https://grafana.redwoodinference.net/d/hosted-latency?var=region=us-east  | Canary dashboard https://grafana.redwoodinference.net/d/runtime-canary?var=pool=rt-1-22-canary

Sean Gallagher: seeing it. p50 fine, p95/p99 spiky. mostly decode tail.

Rafael Mendes: oncall here. impact looks limited to canary cohort? routing says ~6% of traffic.

Allison Grant: quick look at metrics: `kv_fragmentation_ratio` jumped from ~0.18 -> 0.41 in rt-1-22-canary at same time as p99 spike.

Omar El-Sayed: that ratio crossing 0.35 is our compaction trigger default. if it’s thrashing, we could be moving too much per step.

Sofia Ivanova: scheduler view: decode loop showing intermittent stalls ~40-90ms. that’s consistent w/ compaction budget being consumed on the hot path.

Samir Qureshi: checking compaction counters now.

Samir Qureshi: in canary pool: `kv_compaction_bytes_moved_total` is ramping fast. seeing ~18–22GB/min during the spike window. `kv_compaction_step_time_ms` p99 is ~14ms (too high).

Sean Gallagher: that’s huge. any eviction happening?

Allison Grant: eviction counters: `kv_evictions_total{reason="oom_prevent"}` basically flat. `reason="tier_pressure"` small. so mostly compaction not eviction.

Rafael Mendes: so likely compaction overhead is causing tail? what’s the mitigation path — reduce budget or kill-switch?

Omar El-Sayed: safest immediate is flip kill-switch for canary: `runtime.kv.compaction.enabled=false` and/or set budget to near-zero.

Paula Mendes: for canary automation: we can also drop canary weight to 0 while we confirm. what’s preferred?

Sean Gallagher: drop weight to 0 now, then decide if we keep compaction off or tune. we need tail back under SLO.

Rafael Mendes: ack. executing rollback of canary traffic. @DeployBot can you reduce rt-1-22-canary weight to 0 in us-east?

DeployBot: Received request from Rafael Mendes — updating routing policy `hosted-us-east/runtime`:
- pool `rt-1-22-canary` weight: 6 -> 0
ETA: 2-3 minutes

Mateo Alvarez: looping in. please make sure we capture the correlation window + metrics snapshots before they roll over.

Allison Grant: grabbed snapshots + dashboard annotations. adding note: spike aligns w/ `kv_compaction_step_time_ms` and `kv_compaction_pages_relocated_total` not with GPU util.

Sofia Ivanova: scheduler logs show compaction running at prefill/decode boundary but budget looks like it’s not actually capped by “decode_safe_window”. might be a bug in hook.

Samir Qureshi: yep, I’m seeing compaction steps continue even when `scheduler.phase=decode`. that’s not expected. might be the “catch-up” path.

Omar El-Sayed: that would explain stalls. we intended compaction only at phase transitions or in background worker.

Sean Gallagher: confirming current state: any customer-visible still?

Rafael Mendes: p99 is already coming down as traffic drains. still above SLO but trending.

DeployBot: Routing policy update complete. `rt-1-22-canary` weight now 0 for hosted/us-east.

IncidentBot: Update — Mitigation applied: canary traffic drained (weight set to 0). Monitoring for recovery.

Allison Grant: p99 back within SLO band (still noisy but normal). `kv_compaction_bytes_moved_total` stopped increasing (canary no longer receiving traffic).

Sean Gallagher: good. let’s keep canary off until we patch budget enforcement.

Omar El-Sayed: agree. action items:
1) reproduce on staging w/ mixed-length bursts + churn workload
2) verify scheduler enforcement of compaction budget (no compaction during decode)
3) lower defaults: trigger 0.35 -> 0.45 for hosted canary and budget cap (time + bytes)

Sofia Ivanova: I’ll take (2). likely an edge in `phase_end` signaling.

Samir Qureshi: I’ll take (1) and add logging around compaction entrypoints + caller (scheduler vs bg worker).

Rafael Mendes: for incident: do we also want to flip kill-switch explicitly in config, not just routing? in case traffic accidentally re-enabled.

Sean Gallagher: yes. set `runtime.kv.compaction.enabled=false` for hosted/us-east canary pool (even if weight=0).

Rafael Mendes: doing it now via runtime config.

DeployBot: Config change requested — pool `rt-1-22-canary`:
- `runtime.kv.compaction.enabled`: true -> false
ETA: 1-2 minutes

Paula Mendes: logging this as “rollback criteria met” event for canary policy. will attach dashboards + timestamps.

Mateo Alvarez: thanks. please also note: fragmentation ratio was high because canary had extra churn cohort. we should check if allocator changes increased fragmentation or if compaction made it worse.

Allison Grant: quick compare: baseline pool fragmentation ratio stayed ~0.19 during incident window. canary pool rose quickly. hard to say if it’s workload mix or allocator behavior.

Sean Gallagher: ok. but immediate user impact resolved. leaving SEV-2 open until config flip confirmed + 30m stable.

DeployBot: Config change applied — `runtime.kv.compaction.enabled=false` for `rt-1-22-canary`.

Rafael Mendes: confirmed via metrics: `kv_compaction_active=0`.

IncidentBot: Update — Canary compaction disabled via config. Continuing monitoring.

Sean Gallagher: tail stable 30m, no new alerts. proposing resolve.

Rafael Mendes: +1

Omar El-Sayed: +1 (we’ll open follow-up tickets)

IncidentBot: :white_check_mark: INCIDENT RESOLVED — Hosted API tail latency recovered.
End: 1767736764
Summary: Short-lived p99 spike during runtime-1.22 canary. Correlated with high KV fragmentation and excessive compaction work (bytes moved + step time). Mitigation: drained canary traffic + disabled compaction feature flag in canary pool. Follow-ups assigned to Runtime (budget enforcement + defaults + repro).

title: #eng-oncall: 1767660044
preview:
Rafael Mendes: putting together oncall procedures for KV compaction canary. need crisp guidance: when to flip kill-switch vs just reduce budgets, what dashboards to look at, and who to pull in. pls drop thoughts here.

Sean Gallagher: +1. we should make it very binary for oncall: “is compaction likely the cause” -> “do X”. last canary we spent 20 min debating.

Allison Grant: dashboards first. for compaction specifically we should require checking:
- Hosted API Tail Latency (p95/p99) by route/model
- KV allocator health: fragmentation_ratio, free_list_depth, largest_contig_block_bytes
- compaction: bytes_moved_total, compaction_step_time_ms, compaction_budget_hit_count
- eviction: kv_evictions_total (by reason + tier), pinned_eviction_failures
- GPU: mem_used, allocator_oom_count, request_shed_count

Rafael Mendes: do we have canonical links yet or should I just put the grafana search keywords?

Allison Grant: links are fine if stable. otherwise “search: kv.fragmentation_ratio” etc. (and include metric names exactly)

Benji Okafor: kill-switch knobs we have in runtime config (today):
- runtime.kv.compaction.enabled (bool)
- runtime.kv.compaction.budget_ms_per_tick
- runtime.kv.compaction.max_bytes_per_tick
- runtime.kv.eviction.enabled (bool)
- runtime.kv.eviction.max_evictions_per_min
plus a “safe mode” we discussed: set budget to 0 instead of full disable (lets compaction state machine drain without moving pages). not sure if we landed it.

Samir Qureshi: safe mode exists in latest canary build: if enabled=false, relocation stops immediately but leaves any in-flight relocation to finish current step (we guard with refcounts). if budget_ms=0 but enabled=true, it keeps measuring fragmentation + will schedule zero move work (useful to confirm correlation w/o fully toggling).

Sean Gallagher: oncall guidance should be:
1) first response: reduce compaction budget to 0 (NOT full disable) if p99 spikes and compaction_step_time_ms correlates.
2) if p99 still bad, or we see allocator_oom_count climbing, disable compaction + disable eviction (eviction can also cause churn/extra copies)
3) if OOM / shedding: disable compaction immediately, then page runtime.

Omar El-Sayed: careful: in OOM scenario you might want eviction ON (to prevent hard OOM) but compaction OFF (to reduce copy overhead). depends on what’s triggering. if the OOM is from fragmentation, eviction might *help* while we back off compaction.

Rafael Mendes: can we define “when to keep eviction on” as a rule?

Omar El-Sayed: suggestion:
- If we see rising allocator_oom_count OR request_shed_count due to KV allocation failures -> keep eviction enabled, cap evictions_per_min to avoid thrash.
- If we see quality issues / session resets / elevated “sequence_aborted” (not sure metric name) -> consider eviction off, and we may need runtime to look.

Allison Grant: metrics we have for eviction impact:
- kv.eviction.abort_sequence_total
- kv.eviction.pinned_skip_total
- kv.eviction.prefix_cache_drop_total
not perfect but gives signal.

Sean Gallagher: threshold triggers: can we put numbers? e.g.
- if p99 decode latency +20% for >10m in canary cohort AND compaction bytes moved > baseline by 2x
- or compaction_step_time_ms > 5ms p95 -> action

Paula Mendes: rollout automation is already watching p99 + error rate, but oncall doc should say what triggers manual rollback/disable:
- p99 > 1.25x baseline for 10m (canary)
- request_shed_rate > 0.5% for 5m
- allocator_oom_count > 0 sustained
If any of those + compaction metrics are “active”, kill-switch is authorized.

Mateo Alvarez: authority: SRE/eng-oncall can flip kill-switch in an incident without runtime approval. just notify #eng-runtime and open incident channel. if not an incident, please loop Omar/Samir first.

Rafael Mendes: ok. I’ll put that explicitly: “if paging incident, flip first, ask questions after.”

Benji Okafor: config propagation latency: expect 30-90s to hit all hosts in a pool; dedicated can be slower if control plane is busy. oncall should watch the “config applied” metric (we emit runtime.config.last_applied_ts).

Samir Qureshi: also note: disabling compaction doesn’t reclaim already-fragmented layout immediately. improvement should be “no further spikes from moving pages”. fragmentation_ratio may stay high until churn evens out.

Sean Gallagher: escalation path draft:
- If p99 spike + compaction correlated: page Runtime KV (Omar), then Allocator (Samir)
- If correctness suspicion (bad outputs / crashes in relocation): page Noah Patel
- If GPU copy kernel hot / high SM util during compaction: page Grace Kim (kernels)
- For rollback help / flag propagation issues: page Benji

Rafael Mendes: yes. also add “SRE primary owns incident + comms; runtime owns mitigation + followup PR.”

Allison Grant: add a “what dashboards to screenshot” section for incident notes:
- p99 chart with cohort filter
- fragmentation_ratio + bytes_moved overlay
- eviction rate
- allocator_oom_count + shed

Sean Gallagher: command snippets? makes it easier.

Benji Okafor: examples (internal):
`rwdctl config set --scope=hosted-canary --key runtime.kv.compaction.budget_ms_per_tick --value 0`
`rwdctl config set --scope=hosted-canary --key runtime.kv.compaction.enabled --value false`
`rwdctl config set --scope=hosted-canary --key runtime.kv.eviction.max_evictions_per_min --value 60`
(+ include “verify” command: `rwdctl config get ...`)

Omar El-Sayed: also add “verify in runtime logs”: look for `kv_compaction state=disabled reason=operator` and `compaction_budget_ms=0` lines. ppl trust logs more than dashboards.

Rafael Mendes: perfect. I’ll draft runbook section:
- Triage checklist (5 min)
- Decision tree for budget=0 vs disable vs eviction changes
- Verification steps (dashboard + logs + config applied timestamp)
- Escalations + who can flip

Incident Bot: Reminder: If this is customer-impacting, open an incident in #incidents and attach dashboards + config change log.

Sean Gallagher: +1. also: if we flip kill-switch, add a follow-up task to re-enable w/ smaller budget + canary only. don’t leave it off forever.

Rafael Mendes: ack. I’ll put a “post-mitigation” checklist: (1) file retro note, (2) revert to canary defaults next business day w/ runtime owner, (3) update thresholds if alerts were noisy.

- Verification steps (dashboard + logs + config applied timestamp)
- Escalations + who can flip

Incident Bot: Reminder: If this is customer-impacting, open an incident in #incidents and attach dashboards + config change log.

Sean Gallagher: +1. also: if we flip kill-switch, add a follow-up task to re-enable w/ smaller budget + canary only. don’t leave it off forever.

Rafael Mendes: ack. I’ll put a “post-mitigation” checklist: (1) file retro note, (2) revert to canary defaults next business day w/ runtime owner, (3) update thresholds if alerts were noisy.

title: #architecture: 1767579112
preview:
ArchReviewBot: New architecture review request posted by @Omar El-Sayed. Please review within 48h. Links: Confluence (design) https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/kv-compaction , Draft ADRs https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/adr-compaction-budget + https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/adr-eviction-policy

Omar El-Sayed: Hey folks, requesting arch review feedback on KV compaction allocator changes. Main asks:
1) allocator invariants we should codify (what must never be violated)
2) correctness boundaries for relocation (what we guarantee vs what scheduler must guarantee)
3) rollback plan / kill-switch safety (what happens mid-relocation)

PR is still WIP but design is pretty stable. @Noah Patel can you take first pass?

Noah Patel: Yup. Skimming now. Quick clarifying q: are we allowing relocation of *active* sequences (in decode) or only “paused at boundary” sequences?

Samir Qureshi: Current proposal: relocation only when seq enters “movable” state at scheduler boundary (prefill->decode boundary or between decode steps). We don’t relocate while a decode kernel is reading KV.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. Scheduler hook will mark a batch boundary. We can do it after token emission / before next decode step. For continuous batching, we treat “step” as the scheduling quantum.

Noah Patel: Good. Then the core correctness question becomes: what is the exact handshake between scheduler and allocator during that boundary? Need a crisp invariant like “no kernel holds pointers into KV arena during relocation”. How is that enforced? (epoch, refcount, lock, etc)

Samir Qureshi: Mechanism is per-seq state + epoch barrier. Scheduler sets seq.state = MOVING and increments kv_epoch; decode path checks epoch at step start. We also take a short allocator lock for page table swap.

Noah Patel: Please write the invariants explicitly in doc/ADR. Something like:
- I1: For a given sequence, its KV mapping is only mutated while seq.state ∈ {MOVING, EVICTING}.
- I2: A seq can enter MOVING only when it is not present in any in-flight batch (no outstanding GPU work referencing its KV).
- I3: After relocation, mapping swap is atomic wrt readers at step boundary (single pointer swap / generation bump).

Also define what “not present in any in-flight batch” means in your runtime (CPU-side queue vs GPU completion).

Julian Moore: +1 on “GPU completion” nuance. Our trace spans today show decode launch->completion can overlap next scheduler tick for other seqs. Need explicit sync point for the moving seq.

Sofia Ivanova: Implementation detail: we already have per-batch CUDA event tracking for streaming. For MOVING seq we’ll wait on its last decode event (not global sync). That’s in ENG-4824 notes.

Noah Patel: Great. Then I’d like to see a statement: “Relocation requires waiting on last_decode_event(seq)”. Put that in correctness boundary.

Omar El-Sayed: Makes sense. We’ll add it to design + tests.

Noah Patel: Next: allocator invariants.
What’s the unit of allocation? pages/blocks? And are we guaranteeing no partial block moves?

Samir Qureshi: Arena is split into fixed-size pages (currently 64KB). Seqs have a page list per layer/head. Compaction moves whole pages and rewrites the mapping table. We never move partial pages.

Noah Patel: Then add:
- I4: A page is owned by exactly one seq at a time OR in free list; no shared pages.
- I5: Free list correctness: a page can appear in free list at most once; transitions are linearizable.
- I6: Mapping table is source of truth; any cached pointer must be derivable + invalidated on epoch bump.

Also please define what happens with “prefix cache” pages if applicable. Are they under same allocator? (I saw mention of cached prefixes being preemptible.)

Omar El-Sayed: Prefix cache uses same arena but separate namespace/owner type. We tagged those pages as owner=PREFIX_CACHE and have separate eviction priority. Compaction can move them too, but eviction can drop them.

Lena Fischer: On correctness boundaries: eviction of prefix cache is fine, eviction of active sequences is not (unless scheduler cancels seq). We should be explicit that “eviction” for sequences = “preempt” which is a higher-level decision.

Noah Patel: Exactly. Language matters. I’d suggest:
- “Eviction” only applies to cacheable artifacts (prefix cache, speculative prefill results)
- “Preemption” applies to sequences, and requires scheduler-level cancellation semantics + client-visible behavior

Do you ever “evict” a sequence’s KV to CPU? If not, keep it out of scope.

Omar El-Sayed: Not in this phase. Only drop prefix cache / low-priority cached artifacts. Seqs are either kept resident or rejected at admission.

Mateo Alvarez: +1 keep it narrow. If we say “evict sequences” it’ll get misread as swap-to-host.

Allison Grant: Observability angle: please also define invariant around metrics emission during rollback. If kill-switch flips mid-move, what metrics do we expect? (don’t want half-updated counters)

Samir Qureshi: Counters are per-step. We increment “bytes_moved” after successful mapping swap + verification. If abort, we record “compaction_aborts_total” and don’t add bytes moved.

Noah Patel: Good. On rollback plan / kill-switch:
- Can kill-switch be flipped at runtime without restart?
- What happens if it’s flipped while a seq is MOVING?

Omar El-Sayed: Kill-switch is config propagated via control-plane; runtime reads it every N seconds (default 5s). Behavior:
- If disabled, scheduler stops selecting new relocation work.
- If a relocation is in progress, we complete the current “page copy + swap” step then stop (don’t start a new step).

Noah Patel: That’s sane. But you need to articulate the state machine so “stop” doesn’t leave dangling pages.
Propose explicit guarantee:
- G1: At any point during relocation, there exists exactly one valid mapping (either old or new). We do not publish the new mapping until copy is complete.
- G2: If disabled mid-step, allocator reverts to old mapping (or continues to new) but never leaves mixed mapping.

How do you prevent partial publish? (I assume a single mapping pointer swap.)

Samir Qureshi: Yes, mapping swap is a single atomic pointer update to per-seq page table + generation bump. Copy occurs into fresh pages, then we flip. Only after flip we free old pages.

Julian Moore: Do we have a post-copy verification? (like checksum?) Probably too expensive, but maybe a debug mode.

Samir Qureshi: In debug builds we can optionally run a lightweight sanity check (page table sizes + bounds) and poison old pages before free. Not checksumming content.

Noah Patel: That’s fine; document it as “debug-only guardrail”.

Sofia Ivanova: For scheduler boundary, we also need to mention we won’t relocate if the seq is in “STREAMING_PARTIAL” state (client is mid-stream) unless we have the event barrier. But we do have per-seq event barrier, so it should be ok. Still, I’ll add a note that relocation is disallowed when we can’t attach an event (legacy path).

Noah Patel: Please call that out as a correctness boundary: “compaction requires CUDA event tracking enabled”. If there are fallback codepaths, they should opt-out.

Lena Fischer: Backpressure integration: if fragmentation high and compaction budget exhausted, we may still OOM. What’s the policy? We should ensure admission control triggers before allocator hard OOM.

Omar El-Sayed: Proposed: if frag_ratio > threshold AND free_pages < min_free_pages, we signal scheduler “KV pressure” which tightens admission and can trigger prefix cache eviction first. Compaction is best-effort, not the only safety.

Noah Patel: Good. Make that explicit under “correctness boundaries”: compaction is not relied on for safety; admission control remains primary OOM prevention.

Allison Grant: On rollout/rollback criteria: do we have a hard metric-based rollback? (p99 spike correlated with compaction time)

Omar El-Sayed: Yes, SRE is defining it, but initial suggestion: rollback if p99 decode latency +15% for 10m AND compaction_time_ms_p99 > budget AND eviction_rate > threshold. Still tuning.

Mateo Alvarez: We’ll put exact thresholds in SRE guardrails doc. Design doc should just state: “feature flagged, canary, auto-rollback wired to tail latency + compaction metrics.”

Noah Patel: Last request: tests.
Please add a stress test that hits cancellation during relocation (classic hazard). Even if scheduler sets MOVING only at boundaries, cancellations can happen asynchronously.

Samir Qureshi: Already on the list (ENG-4827). We’ll model: cancel arrives after copy but before mapping swap; and cancel after swap but before freeing old pages.

Noah Patel: Perfect. Summarizing review feedback:
- Write explicit invariants I1-I6 + guarantees G1-G2 in doc
- Define boundary conditions: requires per-seq GPU completion event; opt-out legacy paths
- Clarify vocabulary: eviction=cache artifacts; sequences are never evicted (only admitted/rejected/cancelled)
- Kill-switch semantics: stop selecting new work; finish current step; never publish partial mapping
- Add cancellation stress tests around swap/free window

Once those are in, I’m comfortable with the architecture direction.

Omar El-Sayed: Awesome, thanks. I’ll update the Confluence page + ADR text and ping back for final sign-off.

Noah Patel: Sounds good. :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: +1, please tag me when metric definitions are finalized so we can align names with the standard.

Omar El-Sayed: Will do.

title: #eng-runtime: 1767480550
preview:
Omar El-Sayed: Need eyes on the proposed eviction policy before I turn it into the ADR. Goal: when allocator hits pressure (or frag-trigger), we evict cached KV to free contiguous pages rather than thrash/oom. Proposal is tier-aware LRU (sequence-level) + prefix-cache interaction rules. Questions: pinned sessions, and whether prefix KV should be in the eviction pool at all.

Samir Qureshi: can you paste the sketch of the policy? esp what you mean by “tier-aware” (Hosted vs Dedicated) and what object is LRU’d (seq vs block)

Omar El-Sayed: yup. current draft:
- Maintain LRU list per “eviction class” keyed by (tier, pool)
- Eviction classes ordered by priority: Free/Trial Hosted < Paid Hosted < Dedicated burst < Dedicated pinned < Private (no eviction unless explicitly enabled)
- Evict whole sequences’ KV allocations (not individual blocks) to avoid partial state
- Don’t evict ACTIVE sequences (currently decoding/prefill) — only idle ones
- For prefix cache: prefixes are treated as “preemptible cached seqs” with their own class at bottom (i.e., evict prefixes before paid hosted idle chats)

Noah Patel: “evict whole sequence” is good for correctness, but need to be precise: what does eviction mean for an idle sequence? do we fail subsequent decode? do we restart from prompt? I assume this is for shared compute / non-pinned sessions where we can return an error upstream?

Omar El-Sayed: for idle sequences (i.e., session that might continue later): eviction means we drop KV for that session. If it gets a new token later, the scheduler treats it as cache-miss and recomputes from prompt (or refuses if prompt not available). For Hosted chat we *usually* have the full transcript/prompt, so recompute is possible; for some internal streaming modes we may not. Those would be “pinned” / non-evictable.

Sofia Ivanova: +1 we need crisp invariants for scheduler. “pinned” in scheduler today basically means “don’t migrate / don’t preempt”. If eviction tries to touch those, I’d rather we backpressure earlier instead of surprise recompute.

Lena Fischer: from overload/backpressure angle: if we can’t allocate due to pinned / high priority, we should shed low-tier requests *before* we evict something user-visible for paid/dedicated. so ordering matters.

Allison Grant: also for observability: eviction counters need labels by tier + reason (pressure vs frag-compaction vs emergency). otherwise we’re blind when p99 goes weird.

Samir Qureshi: I’m a bit nervous about “prefix cache at bottom”. prefix cache is supposed to be a win for latency; if we always evict it first under pressure, we’re basically turning it off during bursts. maybe that’s ok but we should be explicit.

Marcus Reed: in the mixed-length burst scenario, prefix cache hit rate drops anyway because churn. but if eviction nukes it, our “after” graphs might look worse than baseline. we should decide what we want to optimize for: tail vs average.

Mateo Alvarez: optimize for tail + stability first. prefix cache is nice-to-have if it’s fighting with serving stability. but we should still avoid pathological thrash.

Noah Patel: correctness point: prefix cache isn’t just “nice-to-have” if it’s referenced by active sequences. you need refcounting. if seq A is using prefix P as its first N blocks, you can’t evict P until A is done or has its own copy.

Omar El-Sayed: yep. prefix cache entries are currently “shared prefixes” with refcount > 0 while attached. eviction only considers prefixes with refcount==0 (fully detached). for attached prefixes, they’re effectively part of the sequence’s KV allocation.

Samir Qureshi: ok, but implementationally: do we actually have a single object that represents “sequence KV” vs “prefix KV”? because allocator sees pages. if you say “evict whole sequence”, you need a stable mapping from seq->pages.

Omar El-Sayed: we do: each seq has a handle with a list of KV blocks. prefix cache entry is another handle. attached prefix means seq handle contains pointers to prefix-owned blocks but marked “shared”. eviction path will only take handles where all blocks are owned (or shared w/ refcount==0).

Sofia Ivanova: wait, “shared w/ refcount==0” is weird. if refcount==0 it shouldn’t be shared.

Omar El-Sayed: sorry sloppy wording. for eviction: seq eviction only allowed if it doesn’t reference any shared blocks. prefix eviction only allowed if its refcount==0. we never evict a seq that references shared blocks (because that implies it’s active or at least still logically attached).

Noah Patel: that implies if a seq is idle but still attached to a shared prefix, we *won’t* evict it. is that intended? because that might block eviction and we’ll keep more memory pinned.

Omar El-Sayed: good catch. alternative is: on eviction, we can detach the seq from the shared prefix by “materializing” its prefix blocks (copy-on-write) OR by treating the shared prefix as part of the seq for eviction purposes (i.e., evicting the prefix even if refcount>0 by force-detach). Both are risky.

Samir Qureshi: force-detach is scary. materialize is expensive and defeats the purpose.

Mateo Alvarez: I’d rather be conservative. If idle seq is attached to shared prefix, classify it as non-evictable (for now) and rely on backpressure / evict other things.

Lena Fischer: +1. we can add a metric for “eviction skipped due to shared-prefix attachment” and see if it’s common. if it’s rare, fine.

Marcus Reed: in practice, the prefix cache is most used by short-lived sessions. those sequences end quickly and refcounts go back to 0. I don’t expect tons of idle-but-attached.

Allison Grant: if we’re going conservative, please make sure the “skipped” metric includes a breakdown so we don’t misinterpret low eviction counts as “healthy”.

Benji Okafor: config/flags question: do we want separate knobs for (1) allow eviction at all, (2) allow eviction of prefix cache, (3) allow eviction of paid tiers? I’m thinking runtime flags like:
- kv_eviction_enabled
- kv_eviction_allow_prefix_cache
- kv_eviction_min_tier (free|paid|dedicated|none)

Omar El-Sayed: yes. I was thinking a single “min_tier” gate plus a boolean for prefix cache since it’s not exactly a customer tier. Something like:
- min_evictable_class = hosted_free by default
- prefix_cache_evictable = true by default for Hosted, false for Dedicated?

Sofia Ivanova: for Dedicated, “prefix cache” is still used but it’s mostly within a single customer pool, right? If so, evicting it hurts only that customer, not neighbors. Might be ok, but Dedicated folks care about p99 too.

Noah Patel: pinned sessions: can we define them sharply? I want: pinned == cannot be evicted and cannot be compacted in a way that changes address unless relocation is atomic. otherwise this becomes a correctness bug farm.

Samir Qureshi: pinned in allocator already means “don’t move these pages during compaction” (we added a pin bit on blocks in the draft). eviction should respect the same pin bit.

Omar El-Sayed: proposal update:
- Use the same pin bit: pinned blocks are excluded from both compaction relocation candidates and eviction handles
- Pinned sessions should be *admitted* only if headroom exists; otherwise reject at admission (scheduler) rather than later eviction.

Lena Fischer: admission control path exists. we can add “kv_pin_reservation_bytes” per pool and fail fast if below threshold.

Mateo Alvarez: ok. last issue: what’s the exact ordering? I don’t want “Dedicated burst” evicted before “Paid Hosted” if Dedicated is paying for reserved.

Omar El-Sayed: ordering I’ll put in ADR:
1) Hosted Free/Trial idle seq
2) Hosted background (evals/internal) idle seq
3) Hosted Paid idle seq
4) Prefix cache entries w/ refcount==0 (configurable: can be moved earlier/later)
5) Dedicated *preemptible* idle seq (explicit flag)
Never: Dedicated pinned, Private by default

Sofia Ivanova: makes sense. one nuance: prefix cache maybe should be #1 actually (evictable + doesn’t create user-visible recompute failures, just cache miss). But it can increase load and cause worse tail in the next minute. tradeoff.

Marcus Reed: we can measure. if we evict prefix cache first, we might see throughput drop because more prefill. but tail might still improve due to avoiding OOM/fragmentation. I can add a benchmark toggle for “prefix eviction first vs last”.

Allison Grant: if you do that, please emit a config tag in metrics so we can compare cohorts.

Noah Patel: can we commit to: prefix eviction only when pressure is “hard” (allocation failure imminent), not on “soft” frag-trigger? i.e., compaction handles soft, eviction handles hard.

Samir Qureshi: +1. eviction is a bigger hammer.

Omar El-Sayed: I like that. So:
- Soft trigger (frag_ratio > X): compaction only
- Hard trigger (alloc fail / watermark): eviction, starting with hosted_free + background; prefix cache eviction only if still can’t satisfy request OR if “hard_prefix_ok” flag set

Mateo Alvarez: good. write it up like that.

Omar El-Sayed: will update ADR draft and post for review. TODOs I’m taking:
- define eviction reason codes (HARD_ALLOC_FAIL, HARD_WATERMARK, MANUAL)
- clarify pinned semantics (shared w/ compaction pin bit)
- add “skip reasons” metrics for refcount/pin

Allison Grant: and please add metric names you plan to use. I’ll sanity check against our perf standards.

Sofia Ivanova: tag me in the doc. also, make sure scheduler gets a signal when eviction happened so it can annotate traces + avoid repeating eviction every tick.

Omar El-Sayed: yep, I’ll add “eviction cooldown” in scheduler hook discussion. thanks all.

Benji Okafor: once you land on knobs, tell me and I’ll make sure config propagation supports them (and kill switch can flip fast).

Omar El-Sayed: :thumbsup: will do

- Don’t evict ACTIVE sequences (currently decoding/prefill) — only idle ones
- For prefix cache: prefixes are treated as “preemptible cached seqs” with their own class at bottom (i.e., evict prefixes before paid hosted idle chats)

Noah Patel: “evict whole sequence” is good for correctness, but need to be precise: what does eviction mean for an idle sequence? do we fail subsequent decode? do we restart from prompt? I assume this is for shared compute / non-pinned sessions where we can return an error upstream?

Omar El-Sayed: for idle sequences (i.e., session that might continue later): eviction means we drop KV for that session. If it gets a new token later, the scheduler treats it as cache-miss and recomputes from prompt (or refuses if prompt not available). For Hosted chat we *usually* have the full transcript/prompt, so recompute is possible; for some internal streaming modes we may not. Those would be “pinned” / non-evictable.

Sofia Ivanova: +1 we need crisp invariants for scheduler. “pinned” in scheduler today basically means “don’t migrate / don’t preempt”. If eviction tries to touch those, I’d rather we backpressure earlier instead of surprise recompute.

Lena Fischer: from overload/backpressure angle: if we can’t allocate due to pinned / high priority, we should shed low-tier requests *before* we evict something user-visible for paid/dedicated. so ordering matters.

Allison Grant: also for observability: eviction counters need labels by tier + reason (pressure vs frag-compaction vs emergency). otherwise we’re blind when p99 goes weird.

Samir Qureshi: I’m a bit nervous about “prefix cache at bottom”. prefix cache is supposed to be a win for latency; if we always evict it first under pressure, we’re basically turning it off during bursts. maybe that’s ok but we should be explicit.

Marcus Reed: in the mixed-length burst scenario, prefix cache hit rate drops anyway because churn. but if eviction nukes it, our “after” graphs might look worse than baseline. we should decide what we want to optimize for: tail vs average.

Mateo Alvarez: optimize for tail + stability first. prefix cache is nice-to-have if it’s fighting with serving stability. but we should still avoid pathological thrash.

Noah Patel: correctness point: prefix cache isn’t just “nice-to-have” if it’s referenced by active sequences. you need refcounting. if seq A is using prefix P as its first N blocks, you can’t evict P until A is done or has its own copy.

Omar El-Sayed: yep. prefix cache entries are currently “shared prefixes” with refcount > 0 while attached. eviction only considers prefixes with refcount==0 (fully detached). for attached prefixes, they’re effectively part of the sequence’s KV allocation.

Samir Qureshi: ok, but implementationally: do we actually have a single object that represents “sequence KV” vs “prefix KV”? because allocator sees pages. if you say “evict whole sequence”, you need a stable mapping from seq->pages.

Omar El-Sayed: we do: each seq has a handle with a list of KV blocks. prefix cache entry is another handle. attached prefix means seq handle contains pointers to prefix-owned blocks but marked “shared”. eviction path will only take handles where all blocks are owned (or shared w/ refcount==0).

Sofia Ivanova: wait, “shared w/ refcount==0” is weird. if refcount==0 it shouldn’t be shared.

Omar El-Sayed: sorry sloppy wording. for eviction: seq eviction only allowed if it doesn’t reference any shared blocks. prefix eviction only allowed if its refcount==0. we never evict a seq that references shared blocks (because that implies it’s active or at least still logically attached).

Noah Patel: that implies if a seq is idle but still attached to a shared prefix, we *won’t* evict it. is that intended? because that might block eviction and we’ll keep more memory pinned.

Omar El-Sayed: good catch. alternative is: on eviction, we can detach the seq from the shared prefix by “materializing” its prefix blocks (copy-on-write) OR by treating the shared prefix as part of the seq for eviction purposes (i.e., evicting the prefix even if refcount>0 by force-detach). Both are risky.

Samir Qureshi: force-detach is scary. materialize is expensive and defeats the purpose.

Mateo Alvarez: I’d rather be conservative. If idle seq is attached to shared prefix, classify it as non-evictable (for now) and rely on backpressure / evict other things.

Lena Fischer: +1. we can add a metric for “eviction skipped due to shared-prefix attachment” and see if it’s common. if it’s rare, fine.

Marcus Reed: in practice, the prefix cache is most used by short-lived sessions. those sequences end quickly and refcounts go back to 0. I don’t expect tons of idle-but-attached.

Allison Grant: if we’re going conservative, please make sure the “skipped” metric includes a breakdown so we don’t misinterpret low eviction counts as “healthy”.

Benji Okafor: config/flags question: do we want separate knobs for (1) allow eviction at all, (2) allow eviction of prefix cache, (3) allow eviction of paid tiers? I’m thinking runtime flags like:
- kv_eviction_enabled
- kv_eviction_allow_prefix_cache
- kv_eviction_min_tier (free|paid|dedicated|none)

Omar El-Sayed: yes. I was thinking a single “min_tier” gate plus a boolean for prefix cache since it’s not exactly a customer tier. Something like:
- min_evictable_class = hosted_free by default
- prefix_cache_evictable = true by default for Hosted, false for Dedicated?

Sofia Ivanova: for Dedicated, “prefix cache” is still used but it’s mostly within a single customer pool, right? If so, evicting it hurts only that customer, not neighbors. Might be ok, but Dedicated folks care about p99 too.

Noah Patel: pinned sessions: can we define them sharply? I want: pinned == cannot be evicted and cannot be compacted in a way that changes address unless relocation is atomic. otherwise this becomes a correctness bug farm.

Samir Qureshi: pinned in allocator already means “don’t move these pages during compaction” (we added a pin bit on blocks in the draft). eviction should respect the same pin bit.

Omar El-Sayed: proposal update:
- Use the same pin bit: pinned blocks are excluded from both compaction relocation candidates and eviction handles
- Pinned sessions should be *admitted* only if headroom exists; otherwise reject at admission (scheduler) rather than later eviction.

Lena Fischer: admission control path exists. we can add “kv_pin_reservation_bytes” per pool and fail fast if below threshold.

Mateo Alvarez: ok. last issue: what’s the exact ordering? I don’t want “Dedicated burst” evicted before “Paid Hosted” if Dedicated is paying for reserved.

Omar El-Sayed: ordering I’ll put in ADR:
1) Hosted Free/Trial idle seq
2) Hosted background (evals/internal) idle seq
3) Hosted Paid idle seq
4) Prefix cache entries w/ refcount==0 (configurable: can be moved earlier/later)
5) Dedicated *preemptible* idle seq (explicit flag)
Never: Dedicated pinned, Private by default

Sofia Ivanova: makes sense. one nuance: prefix cache maybe should be #1 actually (evictable + doesn’t create user-visible recompute failures, just cache miss). But it can increase load and cause worse tail in the next minute. tradeoff.

Marcus Reed: we can measure. if we evict prefix cache first, we might see throughput drop because more prefill. but tail might still improve due to avoiding OOM/fragmentation. I can add a benchmark toggle for “prefix eviction first vs last”.

Allison Grant: if you do that, please emit a config tag in metrics so we can compare cohorts.

Noah Patel: can we commit to: prefix eviction only when pressure is “hard” (allocation failure imminent), not on “soft” frag-trigger? i.e., compaction handles soft, eviction handles hard.

Samir Qureshi: +1. eviction is a bigger hammer.

Omar El-Sayed: I like that. So:
- Soft trigger (frag_ratio > X): compaction only
- Hard trigger (alloc fail / watermark): eviction, starting with hosted_free + background; prefix cache eviction only if still can’t satisfy request OR if “hard_prefix_ok” flag set

Mateo Alvarez: good. write it up like that.

Omar El-Sayed: will update ADR draft and post for review. TODOs I’m taking:
- define eviction reason codes (HARD_ALLOC_FAIL, HARD_WATERMARK, MANUAL)
- clarify pinned semantics (shared w/ compaction pin bit)
- add “skip reasons” metrics for refcount/pin

Allison Grant: and please add metric names you plan to use. I’ll sanity check against our perf standards.

Sofia Ivanova: tag me in the doc. also, make sure scheduler gets a signal when eviction happened so it can annotate traces + avoid repeating eviction every tick.

Omar El-Sayed: yep, I’ll add “eviction cooldown” in scheduler hook discussion. thanks all.

Benji Okafor: once you land on knobs, tell me and I’ll make sure config propagation supports them (and kill switch can flip fast).

Omar El-Sayed: :thumbsup: will do

title: #eng-runtime: 1767311205
preview:
Omar El-Sayed: Kicking off kv fragmentation investigation thread. We need a baseline before we touch allocator/compaction. Can folks drop (1) reproduction workload, (2) allocator snapshot output, (3) initial KPI proposal (what we track + target)?

Marcus Reed: I can run the mixed-length churn workload we’ve used for decode tail spikes (the “bursty chat” profile). Question: do we already have a snapshot tool or are we printing internal state?

Samir Qureshi: We have a debug dump behind `RUNTIME_KV_ARENA_DUMP=1` (staging only). It prints page map + free list buckets + a couple summary stats. I’ll paste a sample from last night.

Samir Qureshi: snapshot (A100, llama-2-70b-ish config, 80 req/s, mixed 64-2k prompt, decode up to 512 tokens). After ~25 min steady load + 3 burst cycles:
```
kv_arena:
  page_size_bytes=2097152
  total_pages=1536
  used_pages=1392
  free_pages=144
  free_list_buckets=[0:12, 1:7, 2:5, 4:4, 8:3, 16:2, 32:1, 64:0, 128:0]
  largest_free_run_pages=9
  alloc_fail_soft=27
  alloc_fail_hard=0
  evict_count=0
  relocate_count=0

frag_summary:
  free_bytes=301989888
  free_runs=102
  avg_free_run_bytes=2960685
  largest_free_run_bytes=18874368
  fragmentation_ratio=0.9375

notes:
  cancellations=412
  seq_end=18493
```
Fragmentation ratio here is `1 - (largest_free_run / total_free)` (so higher = worse). Looks nasty: largest run is ~18MB out of ~288MB free.

Marcus Reed: Whoa. That aligns with the “can’t allocate contiguous” stalls we saw? `alloc_fail_soft=27` is the re-try path right?

Samir Qureshi: yup. “soft” == we had enough total free pages but couldn’t satisfy the run-length needed for the next KV extend without walking/merging for a while. That’s where we see occasional 10-40ms spikes in the allocator critical section.

Sofia Ivanova: Can we correlate this snapshot with p99 decode? Like grab a snapshot right before/after the spikes?

Julian Moore: I can add a quick log hook to emit a snapshot when `alloc_fail_soft > 0` in a 10s window + include current scheduler phase (prefill vs decode). But we should keep it staging-only or rate-limited.

Allison Grant: Before we go too far: we need to agree on metric definitions (names + semantics). I’m seeing “fragmentation_ratio” in Samir’s dump, but we should standardize what it means. Also we probably need at least: free_bytes, largest_free_run, bytes_moved (once compaction exists), compaction_time_ms, eviction_count, and a “compaction_active” flag.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. For now I’m mostly looking for baseline numbers and a KPI target we can socialize (runtime + SRE). Samir, can you share a second snapshot from a “healthy” state (fresh boot / low churn) so we have a delta?

Samir Qureshi: same config, 2 min after start (before burst):
```
kv_arena:
  total_pages=1536
  used_pages=812
  free_pages=724
  largest_free_run_pages=662
frag_summary:
  free_bytes=1518338048
  largest_free_run_bytes=1387266048
  fragmentation_ratio=0.0864
alloc_fail_soft=0
```
So we go from ~0.09 to ~0.94 after churn.

Sean Gallagher: jumping in from SRE side. What tail metric are we anchoring this to? For Hosted, folks keep quoting p99 end-to-end. If this is allocator-induced, we should track a runtime-internal latency too (e.g., “decode step time” or “scheduler loop duration”) so we can distinguish network/queueing.

Marcus Reed: For perf suite I can capture:
- p50/p95/p99 end-to-end request latency
- p95/p99 time-to-first-token
- decode token latency distribution
Also can isolate “allocator critical section time” if we have a span/counter.

Omar El-Sayed: Proposed KPIs (v0):
1) Fragmentation: keep `fragmentation_ratio` <= 0.25 in steady state under the mixed-length churn workload (after warmup + 3 bursts).
2) Tail latency: under same workload, p99 decode token time regression <= +3% vs baseline (and ideally improve by >=10% on the burst scenario).
3) Safety: hard alloc fails / OOMs = 0; correctness test suite green.
4) Overhead: compaction CPU/GPU copy time <= 1% wall clock (or budgeted so it can’t spike decode).
Feedback?

Allison Grant: On #1: ratio threshold is fine but we also need absolute numbers. Ratio can look “good” when free_bytes is tiny (near-full arena). Suggest we emit both:
- `kv_free_bytes`
- `kv_largest_free_run_bytes`
- `kv_fragmentation_ratio = 1 - largest_free_run_bytes / max(free_bytes, 1)`
And alerting uses ratio only when free_bytes > some minimum.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Also for rollout gating I want a single “allocator health” panel: ratio + soft_fail_rate + time_in_alloc_ms. If soft_fail_rate goes up, we roll back.

Sofia Ivanova: For the overhead KPI: we should enforce compaction only at safe boundaries (prefill/decode boundary) or with a strict per-iteration budget. If we try to compact mid-decode we’ll create the exact spikes we’re trying to eliminate.

Samir Qureshi: Agree. Also note: the run-length requirement grows with sequence length. When you get a couple long-lived chats, their KV extends tend to request larger contiguous chunks (depending on how we pack blocks). That’s why `largest_free_run` matters.

Marcus Reed: Can we define the “mixed-length churn workload” concretely so we’re not each running something slightly different? Here’s what I’ve been using:
- 70% sessions: prompt 64-256 tokens, decode 128-256
- 25%: prompt 256-1024, decode 256-512
- 5%: prompt 1024-2048, decode 256-512
- churn: 20% cancellation rate mid-stream
- bursts: 2x QPS for 90s every 8 min
I can check this into benchmark-lab if we agree.

Omar El-Sayed: Yes, let’s lock that in as “frag-burst-v1”. Marcus please write it up + link here.

Mateo Alvarez: (catching up) This is great. Two asks:
1) Make sure KPI is customer-aligned: “reduce p99 spikes during bursts” should be the headline.
2) Don’t overfit to a single model/GPU. At least run A100 + H100 + one smaller model config.

Marcus Reed: Ack. I’ll run A100/H100 for llama-70b profile + a smaller 13b-ish profile (whatever our standard perf harness uses).

Allison Grant: Naming proposal for metrics (so dashboards aren’t a mess):
- `runtime.kv.free_bytes`
- `runtime.kv.largest_free_run_bytes`
- `runtime.kv.fragmentation_ratio`
- `runtime.kv.alloc_fail_soft_total`
- `runtime.kv.alloc_fail_hard_total`
Later:
- `runtime.kv.compaction.bytes_moved_total`
- `runtime.kv.compaction.time_ms_total`
- `runtime.kv.eviction.total`
- `runtime.kv.eviction.reason{tier,reason}`
Please don’t bake “A100/llama70b” into metric names; use labels.

Samir Qureshi: works for me. Also we should emit `runtime.kv.free_runs` (count of free extents). It’s a good proxy for fragmentation even when the ratio is noisy.

Sean Gallagher: +1 on `free_runs`. It tends to correlate with allocator walk time.

Omar El-Sayed: Cool, let’s finalize v0 KPIs:
- Fragmentation health: `fragmentation_ratio` and `free_runs` at end of warm steady state + after each burst. Target: ratio <= 0.25 AND free_runs <= 20 (tunable) when free_bytes >= 256MB.
- Tail latency: p99 decode token time and p99 end-to-end. Target: no regression vs baseline; stretch goal: reduce burst p99 by >=10%.
- Allocator pain signals: soft_fail_rate (per minute) should drop materially (goal: <1/min in the workload).
- Safety: no hard fails/OOM; cancel path correctness.

Marcus Reed: I’ll capture baseline runs today and post:
- timeseries of ratio/free_runs
- p95/p99
- a couple snapshots at spike times

Julian Moore: I can add the “snapshot on soft_fail spike” hook to staging this afternoon. Will keep it behind `RUNTIME_KV_ARENA_DUMP_ON_FAIL=1` and rate-limit to 1/min. Will PR once ready.

Allison Grant: Please also include a short note on how to compute ratio exactly (float, 0..1). We keep getting inverted ratios in dashboards :)

Samir Qureshi: lol yes. I’ll update the dump output to print both `largest_free_run_bytes` and the computed ratio so it’s unambiguous.

Omar El-Sayed: Great. Next step: Marcus baseline link + Samir add metric/dump clarifications. Then we can draft the design doc with real numbers.

Marcus Reed: will post results by EOD. :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: Thanks all. Once you have baseline, ping me and Allison—SRE wants to bake alerts from day 1 of canary.

Omar El-Sayed: will do.
